The fusion of the *bcr* and *abl* genes results from the Philadelphia translocation, creating a chimeric gene known as *bcr-abl*.

No, zolpidem is a hypnotic drug that interacts at the benzodiazepine recognition site and is chemically distinct from benzodiazepines. It is described as having neuropharmacological and behavioral effects related to sleep and sedation, and its effects are reduced by rifampin due to enhanced metabolism, but there is no mention of any antibiotic properties.

Yes, desvenlafaxine can be used at a dose of 50mg/day. It is described as the recommended therapeutic dose, initiated without dose titration, and at this dose, discontinuation rates due to adverse events are comparable to placebo. Studies have also assessed the efficacy and tolerability of desvenlafaxine at 50mg/day in treating major depressive disorder.
Rotor syndrome is linked to deficiencies of OATP1B1 and OATP1B3.

Afatinib is used for non-small cell lung cancer, particularly in patients with advanced disease harboring activating mutations in the EGFR gene or those who have developed resistance to other EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors.

Yes, ferric carboxymaltose can be used to treat anemia in inflammatory bowel disease patients. Several studies analyze its efficacy and safety in this population, demonstrating improvements in iron levels, hemoglobin, and ferritin following treatment, and showing it to be more effective than iron sucrose in some cases. It is used both as a preventative measure against anemia recurrence and to correct existing iron deficiencies.
Eliglustat is used to treat Gaucher disease type 1.
Several algorithms are suitable for predicting protein complexes, including MCL, Spectral, RNSC, Affinity Propagation, NDComplex, ClusPro, GIBA, and a Genetic-Algorithm Fuzzy Naïve Bayes model.

PiggyBac transposons are mobile DNA segments that can insert into genomes, disrupting gene function and enabling insertional mutagenesis. They are a tool for delivering genetic material, including reprogramming factors, and can integrate stably into a target genome without relying on host factors. Furthermore, PiggyBac transposons can mobilize large DNA fragments—even up to 100kb—and are capable of both stable cargo transfer and seamless removal from the genome.

Yes, variants at the ITIH3/4 region have attained genome-wide significance in schizophrenia, and studies confirm associations to this locus in individuals with the disorder. Additionally, the ITIH3 polymorphism rs2535629 showed a significant association with schizophrenia in a study of Japanese subjects.
APOBEC3B protein is predominantly nuclear, though it becomes nuclear after mitosis and can be present in the cytoplasm as well.

No, Vsr endonuclease levels are growth phase dependent, decreasing during the log phase relative to Dcm cytosine methylase. Additionally, very short-patch repair, initiated by Vsr endonuclease, is more efficient during the stationary phase while mismatch repair is more efficient during exponential growth.

Yes, interactomes of POU5F1 and SOX2 enhancers have been assayed in human embryonic stem cells using the 4C-Seq technique, revealing frequent interacting regions that overlap with early DNA replication domains. These interactomes are associated with active histone marks and enriched with 5-hydroxymethylcytosine sites.
CAGEr is a comprehensive CAGE toolbox on a common analysis platform integrated with CAGE data processing and promoterome mining.

Yes, rheumatoid arthritis is related to myopathy. One report details a child with juvenile rheumatoid arthritis developing steroid myopathy during treatment, and another discusses drug-induced myopathy occurring alongside myopathy due to the primary disease in connective tissue disorders like rheumatoid arthritis. Additionally, statin-induced myopathy is a risk for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

GATA-4 functions as an angiogenic survival factor and is required for adaptive responses in the infarcted heart. Delivering GATA-4 via gene transfer increases ejection fraction, fractional shortening, and reduces infarct size, while also increasing capillaries and stemlike cells and decreasing apoptosis. Additionally, GATA-4 is found within a subpopulation of primitive cells from the heart that exhibit proliferative potential and can enhance cardiac repair after implantation into ischemic hearts. These findings suggest GATA-4 plays a role in myocardial regeneration and angiogenesis following myocardial infarction.

Elite endurance and mixed-sports athletes survive longer than the general population, exhibiting lower mortality and higher longevity, while long-term vigorous exercise training is associated with increased survival rates for specific groups of athletes, and regular physical activity is associated with an increase of life expectancy by 0.4 to 6.9 years.

Yes, EZH2 is associated with prostate cancer. It promotes proliferation and invasiveness of prostate cancer cells, correlates with progression to hormone-refractory and metastatic disease, and its overexpression is linked to increased likelihood of therapy failure and disease relapse after radical prostatectomy. It is also amplified and overexpressed in prostate cancer, and increased expression correlates with an adverse prognosis.

Thyroid hormone action can resemble states of catecholamine excess, and an excess of thyroid hormone may increase adrenergic responsiveness in the heart. Isoproterenol, a sympathomimetic drug, upregulates certain thyroid hormone receptor mRNA levels, while amiodarone and T3 downregulate them. In heart failure, a reduction in beta-adrenergic receptors is associated with reduced expression of thyroid hormone receptors.

Computational tools for the prediction of beta-barrel transmembrane proteins include methods based on Bayesian Networks achieving 88.6% strand accuracy, PRED-TMBB—a web server utilizing a Hidden Markov Model—and BTMX, a method to predict residue exposure status with up to 84.2% accuracy, alongside several other tools for identifying potential transmembrane proteins and their regions by comparing query proteins against target models.

AMP-activated protein kinase alpha regulates stress granule biogenesis. Specifically, AMPK-α2 associates with stress granules under stressful conditions, and its activity is linked to their formation. Evidence suggests that AMPK regulates stress granule parameters and interacts with the SG-nucleating protein G3BP1 during stress.

Studies have investigated a possible association between long-term use of mobile and cordless phones and glioma, a type of malignant brain tumor. One study found no significant association between glioma and mobile phone use, while another explored the relationship between long-term wireless phone use and malignant brain tumors. Radiofrequency electromagnetic fields emitted from these phones have been classified as a possible human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer.

Yes, there is cross-talk between the Wnt and Akt pathways. Several instances demonstrate this interaction: Wnt stimulation leads to phosphorylation of Akt, and activation of the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway affects beta-catenin signaling; furthermore, Akt can facilitate activation of beta-catenin, and dysregulation of both pathways synergizes in disease development.

Yes, GFP has been used in transgenesis for live protein imaging. Several studies detail the use of GFP, fused to other proteins, to investigate cell cycles, lineages, and protein localization in live animals and cells, including in Xenopus laevis and transgenic pigs. Additionally, live imaging was performed using GFP fluorescence in C. elegans embryos and to track protein trafficking in Plasmodium falciparum.

Yes, deletions including the SNORD116 cluster are found in patients with Prader-Willi syndrome, and it is implicated as a major contributor to the syndrome’s phenotype. Several instances of deletions encompassing SNORD116 have been identified in individuals exhibiting characteristics of the syndrome, supporting a role for this cluster in its pathogenesis.
Aleglitazar is a dual agonist of peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha/gamma (PPARalpha/gamma).

Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT) is an inherited heart rhythm disorder causing potentially life-threatening arrhythmias during physical or emotional stress. It can lead to syncope, sudden cardiac death, or ventricular fibrillation, often appearing in children and young patients with structurally normal hearts. This condition is linked to mutations in genes like *RYR2* and *CASQ2*, which affect calcium handling within heart muscle cells, and can be inherited through autosomal dominant or recessive patterns.

Yes, poly(ADP-ribosylation) is involved in transcriptional control. It functions as a transcriptional coactivator of nuclear factor kappaB and hypoxia inducible factor 1, and its modification can restrain transcriptional coactivator function and reduce gene expression. Additionally, ADP-ribosylation appears to be involved in the direct induction of gene transcription, as inhibitors of poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase blocked gene expression.

Yes, nimodipine is recommended as it reduces the risk of ischemic brain damage and has been shown to reduce the occurrence of delayed ischemic cerebral dysfunction with subsequent fixed neurological dysfunction in aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage patients. It is described as a principal advance in treatment and is used prophylactically.

Bradykinin and prostaglandins mediate the exudative and inflammatory phases of healing after myocardial infarction. Pharmacological interference with prostaglandin synthesis following myocardial infarction reduced infarct area, a measure of scar thickness, at week 4 in rats. However, this intervention did not reduce collagen accumulation at the site of the myocardial infarction, but it was evident remote to the MI. Therefore, prostaglandins contribute to fibrogenesis and healing processes following myocardial infarction, particularly in areas surrounding the initial injury.

H3K79 methylation is coupled with active gene transcription and correlates with mRNA abundance across diverse mammalian cell types. While different degrees of methylation exist, they appear to function with redundancy, relying on global methylation levels rather than specific states. This modification is found at active genes and can dynamically respond to changes in gene activity, though it is not essential for all gene transcriptions or specific pathways like Wnt signaling.

Lacritin can be found in human salivary gland cells, human corneal epithelial cells, tears from several species, the lacrimal gland, human breast tissues—specifically in 51% of primary invasive breast tumors, 71% of breast cancer cell lines, and 56% of normal breast tissues—and is also secreted by several nongermative epithelia.

Dermcidin is found in eccrine sweat glands and actively participates in the innate immune defense of human skin. It can also be released from cultured cell lines and primary neurons, though neuronal uptake of the protein was not observed. Additionally, dermcidin may be produced in the placenta and is present in maternal peripheral blood mononuclear cells, with increased concentrations of an isoform found in acute myocardial infarction.

The triple screening test measures maternal serum markers such as alpha-fetoprotein, human chorionic gonadotropin, and unconjugated estriol to estimate fetal risk of genetic abnormalities, open neural tube defects, and chromosomal abnormalities like trisomies 13, 18, and 21.

Generic versions of Viagra contain sildenafil citrate and were illegally offered by 90% of websites examined, though often these were counterfeit with varying amounts of the active pharmaceutical ingredient.
SLC38A9 is a physical and functional component of the Ragulator-RAG GTPases machinery that controls the activation of mTOR.
Reverse vaccinology has been applied to Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B, Ehrlichia ruminantium, and is being discussed for potential use against other human pathogens and viral vaccines.
Galassi classification is used for middle cranial fossa arachnoid cysts and sylvian fissure arachnoid cysts.

The Mozart effect refers to improved performance on tests of spatial abilities after listening to music composed by Mozart, specifically pieces like sonatas K.448 and K.545. This effect may be linked to changes in arousal and mood, and appears to be more demonstrable in individuals without musical training. Additionally, listening to Mozart can decrease oscillatory power in the brain and reduce epileptiform discharges in children with epilepsy.

The Tokuhashi scoring system is a prognostic tool used to predict life expectancy in patients with spinal metastases. It was initially formulated with a total of 12 points and later revised to 15 points to improve preoperative prediction of survival time. Determining a patient’s life expectancy is an important factor when selecting the appropriate treatment plan, and the system helps evaluate this prognosis. Modified versions of the Tokuhashi score have demonstrated the highest predictive value for survival among several scoring systems.

Several thyroid hormone analogs exist, including thyromimetics, tiromes, 3,5-diiodothyropropionic acid (DITPA), and eprotirome, with some designed to selectively bind to beta1-type nuclear thyroid hormone receptors while others act as both agonists and antagonists of thyroid hormone receptors.

Yes, lenvatinib demonstrates clinical activity and progression-free survival benefits in patients with radioiodine-refractory thyroid cancer, and was approved for the treatment of progressive, symptomatic, radioactive iodine refractory, advanced differentiated thyroid cancer. It showed a median progression-free survival of 18.3 months compared to 3.6 months with placebo, and a response rate of 64.8%.

Genes related to breast cancer include BRCA2, BRCA1, 16 cancer-related genes and 5 reference genes analyzed by Oncotype DX, 70 genes analyzed by MammaPrint, genes associated with tumor proliferation and invasion, HER-2/neu expression genes, ER expression genes, progesterone receptor (PR) expression genes, MYH9, MYL9, and CYR61.
Denosumab (Prolia) binds to receptor activator of nuclear factor-kappaB ligand (RANKL).

Several fluorescent reporter proteins are mentioned, including red cytoplasmic fluorescent protein, green fluorescent protein, tdTomato, and flavin-based fluorescent proteins derived from Pseudomonas putida, Bacillus subtilis, and Arabidopsis thaliana.

MutT proteins protect bacteria from mutations induced by 8-oxoG lesions in DNA. They achieve this by degrading 8-oxo-7,8-dihydrodeoxyguanosine triphosphate (8-oxo-dGTP), a mutagenic substrate for DNA synthesis, thus preventing DNA replication errors. Additionally, MutT proteins are involved in the prevention of base substitution mutations, and their antimutator effect can be complemented by backup systems in actively growing cells.

Yes, tamoxifen was tested for treatment of glioma patients, both alone and in combination with other therapies, in multiple trials investigating its efficacy and toxicity for recurrent or refractory gliomas in adults and children. Studies explored different dosages and methods of administration, including high-dose oral tamoxifen and its use alongside interferon alpha, irinotecan, propylthiouracil, carboplatin, and radiation therapy.

The four Yamanaka factors used to generate induced pluripotent stem cells are Oct4, Klf4, Sox2, and cMyc, as demonstrated through reprogramming of various somatic cells including fibroblasts, peripheral blood cells, and endometrial adult stromal cells.

Anhedonia is associated with poorer health status and more somatic and cognitive symptoms in coronary artery disease patients. It independently predicts mortality following percutaneous coronary intervention and after acute coronary syndromes, and is a significant predictor of major adverse cardiac events and all-cause mortality, even independent of depressed mood or depressive symptom severity. Experiencing anhedonia appears to indicate increased risk beyond established medical prognostic indicators in these patients.

Naltrexone does not appear to modify motor symptoms in Parkinson’s disease, and has shown minimal effect on levodopa-induced dyskinesias. However, naltrexone can be an effective treatment for pathological gambling and impulse control disorders that may develop in Parkinson’s disease patients, particularly those treated with dopamine agonists. While higher doses may cause hepatic abnormalities, naltrexone is generally well tolerated, and can lead to remission of these behavioral issues when other treatments are ineffective.  It may also lead to a decrease in symptom severity related to impulse control disorders.

Oxaliplatin is an effective chemotherapeutic agent against advanced colorectal cancer, and is commonly used for its treatment around the world. While it shows limited efficacy as a single agent, a recommended dose for oxaliplatin monotherapy is 130 mg/m(2) every 3 weeks. Combining oxaliplatin with other treatments, such as cetuximab, can restore responsiveness in cases of resistance and improve efficacy.

Yes, several animal models are used to analyze impaired pathways in Niemann-Pick type C1 disease, including mutant mice and felines. Additionally, the npc1 mouse is specifically mentioned as a murine model of the disease used in treatment studies.

SATB1 regulates numerous genes during thymocyte differentiation, including interleukin-2 receptor alpha, c-myc, and those encoded by mouse mammary tumor virus, as well as genes regulated by SATB1 within a roughly 10 kb region surrounding its binding site.

No, antitumor activity of trastuzumab deruxtecan is dependent on HER2 expression level, and high HER2 expression leads to sensitivity to this treatment, while cells essentially negative for HER2 expression can be killed in the presence of HER2-expressing cells. Additionally, trastuzumab is designed to bind and inhibit the HER2 receptor and is approved for treatment of HER2-positive cancers.

H19 and lincRNA-p21 are among the most abundant human lincRNAs, with H19 being highly conserved and expressed in both embryonic and extra-embryonic cell lineages, and lincRNA-p21 demonstrating essential roles in hypoxia-enhanced glycolysis and tumor growth.

CCDC40 is a coiled-coil domain containing protein essential for motile cilia function and left-right axis formation. It is required for axonemal recruitment of CCDC39 and is involved in the correct assembly of inner dynein arms and dynein regulatory complexes within cilia. Mutations in CCDC40 lead to defects in cilia motility and can cause primary ciliary dyskinesia, often with axonemal disorganization and absent inner dynein arms.

Yes, thyrotropin-releasing hormone administration appears to reduce fatigue in cancer patients. Studies demonstrate significant improvement in fatigue levels as measured by the visual analog scale for energy, the fatigue and vigor subscales of the profile of mood states, and the fatigue subscale of the Functional Assessment of Chronic Illness Therapy-Fatigue following TRH treatment, and initial findings support its ability to ameliorate cancer-related fatigue.

Chromomethylase Zea methyltransferase2 is required for the in vivo methylation of CpNpG sequences in plants. DNA methylation is a primary mechanism for epigenetic variation and can induce phenotypic alterations in plants under stress. These epigenetic changes, driven by alterations in DNA bases like methylation, contribute to adaptation and tolerance responses to both abiotic and biotic stresses, ultimately altering plant growth and development.

Yes, curcumin is repeatedly described as a phytochemical derived from the rhizome of Curcuma longa, and is noted as being extracted from this plant in multiple instances. It is also referred to as a naturally occurring polyphenolic phytochemical.

Cerebral cavernous malformation is a vascular disease of the brain that can cause headaches, seizures, and cerebral hemorrhage. It exists in both sporadic and familial forms, with familial cases often involving multiple malformations due to an autosomal dominant condition. Mutations in genes like KRIT1 and MGC4607, as well as PDCD10, are associated with the disease, and it can manifest with varying degrees of aggressiveness and additional symptoms like scoliosis or cognitive disability. It involves malformation of cerebral capillaries and can lead to cerebral hemorrhage, strokes, and seizures.

Yes, Fanconi anemia is described as both genetically and phenotypically heterogeneous in multiple instances, characterized by diverse congenital malformations and a variable predisposition to malignancies. It is a recessive disorder with diverse congenital malformations and is genetically heterogeneous, with mutated genes encoding proteins involved in the cellular response to DNA replication stress.
The mTOR pathway, specifically through mTORC1 and mTORC2, regulates SGK1-mediated phosphorylation of FOXO3a to control cell proliferation.

Bortezomib, 17-AAG, digitoxin, staurosporine, digoxin, rubitecan, trimetrexate, and SD-1029 have demonstrated effectiveness in inhibiting chordoma cell growth, with bortezomib showing particular promise in combination therapies with topoisomerase I and II inhibitors.

ALS-linked FUS mutants, wild-type FUS, and PABP are known to be related to motor neuron degeneration in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis through their recruitment to cytoplasmic stress granules, particularly following oxidative stress, which can disrupt RNA processing and initiate neurodegeneration.

Yes, evidence demonstrates the de novo genesis of enhancers in vertebrates, specifically in fish, where regions losing coding capacity but retaining sequence conservation with mammals exhibit enhancer activity while their orthologous coding regions do not. This suggests minor changes in non-regulatory sequences can generate new enhancers, contributing to evolutionary innovation.

Possible causes of hypersomnia include sleep apnea syndrome, narcolepsy, chronic sleep deprivation or insufficiency, restless legs/periodic limb movements in sleep syndrome, medication- and toxin-dependent sleepiness, psychiatric disorders, neurological disorders, posttraumatic conditions, infections, metabolic or endocrine diseases, and central disorders of hypersomnolence such as narcolepsy types 1 and 2 or idiopathic hypersomnia.

Caspases are evolutionarily conserved cysteine proteases that function as critical mediators of apoptosis and effector components of programmed cell death, but also play a role in nonapoptotic functions such as proliferation, differentiation, survival, and the regulation of cytokine expression.

Yes, TNNI3K is consistently described as cardiac-specific throughout the provided information. It is identified as a cardiac-specific kinase, a cardiac-restricted gene, and a novel cardiac-specific protein kinase, with high expression specifically in the heart and interactions primarily with cardiac proteins like cardiac troponin I.

Rif1 regulates DNA replication by opposing the function of DDK, specifically through directing Protein Phosphatase 1 to dephosphorylate the MCM complex. It establishes mid-S replication domains and regulates replication timing programs, impacting when sequences replicate during S-phase. Rif1 also contributes to survival during replication stress and facilitates homology-directed repair at stalled replication forks, and it appears to regulate chromatin loop structures which influence replication timing domain organization. Furthermore, Rif1 directly binds to DNA cruciform structures via its C-terminal region, linking its function to DNA binding.

Yes, factor XI deficiency is the cause of Hemophilia C. Several reports explicitly state that factor XI deficiency is also called Hemophilia C, and it is a deficiency of this protein that characterizes the condition.

Gluten induces diseases like wheat allergy, celiac disease, and gluten sensitivity, and is a common cause of food intolerance affecting approximately 1% of the general population. While celiac disease is an autoimmune response to gluten, gluten sensitivity involves innate immune mechanisms and can present with intestinal and extraintestinal symptoms, sometimes linked to genetic factors beyond the HLADQ2, -DQ8 system. Food intolerances, including those related to gluten, are reactions to foods not caused by immunological mechanisms and can mimic or coexist with functional gastrointestinal disorders like irritable bowel syndrome. Dermatitis herpetiformis is also linked to gluten intolerance and presents as an autoimmune blistering disease.

Yes, mutations of the EDNRB gene have been detected in patients with Hirschsprung’s disease, and impaired function of the endothelin-B receptor is involved in the aetiology of some human cases of the disease. These mutations appear to be associated with short-segment Hirschsprung’s disease, and account for up to 5% of cases in the general population. Several novel mutations have been identified, and disease-causing mutation rates in the gene were estimated at around 10.7%.

Smartphone based Personal Allergy Assistants allow patients to keep an electronic patient diary by scanning food product barcodes. These electronic diaries are regularly transmitted to the allergist’s electronic patient record for diagnostic purposes and can warn patients before consuming allergenic foods. This telemedicine support, combined with Disease Management, may positively impact medical outcomes and health-related costs for food allergy patients. A web-based network has also been established to collect computer readable food ingredient lists needed for this diet management.

Yes, BAX can trigger the release of cytochrome c. Several sources indicate BAX activation leads to cytochrome c release from mitochondria, contributing to apoptosis in various systems including MCF7 cells, neurons infected with reoviruses, and cells exposed to certain treatments. Additionally, it is stated that once activated, BAX forms pores in the outer mitochondrial membrane leading to cytochrome c release.

Endoplasmic Reticulum-Associated Degradation (ERAD) is a quality control process that eliminates aberrant or unwanted proteins from the endoplasmic reticulum. It involves the dislocation of protein substrates from the ER to the cytosol, where they are ubiquitinated and degraded by the proteasome. This process targets misfolded proteins, preventing their deployment to other destinations and ensuring proper protein folding and delivery.

Vemurafenib, dabrafenib, PLX4032, and TAK-632 have been tested in clinical trials for treatment of melanoma patients, with dabrafenib also being investigated in combination with trametinib and PLX8394 showing promise for future trials.

Risk factors for sudden cardiac death in patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy include age, syncope, family history of sudden cardiac death, prior cardiac arrest, nonsustained ventricular tachycardia, abnormal blood pressure response to exercise, high-risk genetic mutations, previous cardiac arrest or sustained ventricular tachycardia, hypotensive response to exercise, marked left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial ischemia determined by exercise thallium scintigraphy, and the presence of myocardial fibrosis as demonstrated by late gadolinium enhancement.

Urinary incontinence imposes a substantial financial burden on individuals, families, and healthcare systems. In 1995, the societal cost of incontinence for those 65 and older was $26.3 billion, or $3565 per person, while in 1998, the total annual cost for Australian women over 18 was estimated at $710.44 million. Costs associated with overactive bladder and stress urinary incontinence were quantified in Korea as 117-145 billion Korean Won and 122-59 billion Korean Won between 2006 and 2007, and estimated at $12.02 billion in the United States in 2000.

Transcriptional co-activator with PDZ-binding motif (TAZ) is a coactivator of the Yes-associated protein (YAP), and together they interact with TEAD transcription factors to activate gene transcription.

Over 30 non-MHC genes have been identified in humans as being genetically associated with Rheumatoid Arthritis through genome-wide association studies, in addition to more than 10 regions identified in rodent models.

Yes, optogenetics is utilized both for studying epilepsy mechanisms and developing potential treatments. It allows for precise control of neuronal activity through light-sensitive proteins, enabling researchers to test causality, identify therapeutic approaches, and even silence neurons that initiate or propagate seizures, particularly in animal models. It is also being explored for influencing future neurosurgical interventions.

Stress-induced mutagenesis occurs in nongrowing cells, contrasting with mutagenesis in actively dividing populations, and transcription-associated mutagenesis is associated with high levels of transcription.

E-cadherin, vimentin, laminin 332, and Par6 are related to the loss of cell-cell adhesion during EMT, as evidenced by their decreased expression or involvement in the dissolution of junctional complexes during the transition to a mesenchymal phenotype, alongside the action of transcriptional repressors like Snail, Slug, and the ZEB family.
Yes, miR-1 influences susceptibility to cardiac arrhythmias after myocardial infarction.

Yes, nematode and fly genomes contain CNEs. These elements share sequence characteristics with vertebrate CNEs, though they lack identity, and are associated with developmental regulatory genes, suggesting a conserved function as cis-regulatory elements across different animal lineages.

The tumor suppressor p53 is frequently referred to as “the guardian of the genome” due to its ability to preserve genome integrity, respond to genotoxic stress, and protect the genome through DNA repair, cell cycle arrest, or apoptosis.

Mutations in the CHCHD10 gene are associated with several diseases including frontotemporal dementia with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, mitochondrial myopathy, and late onset spinal motor neuronopathy. These mutations appear to be autosomal dominant in some cases and can cause a range of symptoms, such as bulbar symptoms, skeletal muscle abnormalities, and lower motor neuron syndrome. The CHCHD10 protein is located in the mitochondria and plays a role in mitochondrial genome stability, maintenance of cristae junctions, and morphologic remodeling of the mitochondria. While infrequent, identifying CHCHD10 mutations is particularly important when bulbar symptoms are present at the onset of disease.

Flumazenil is a specific benzodiazepine antagonist that is useful in reversing the sedation and respiratory depression associated with benzodiazepine overdose and is considered an excellent antidote for this condition.

No, the provided information does not mention the effect of thyroid hormone signaling on microRNA expression. However, it states that many cardiac genes are transcriptionally regulated by T3, and impairment of T3 signaling leads to changes in gene expression that contribute to contractile dysfunction, suggesting a broad influence on gene expression within the heart.

Next-generation sequencing is applied to identify novel diagnostic and rare cancer mutations, as well as detect translocations, insertions, deletions, and copy number variants. It enables the simultaneous detection of all possible variants in genes related to conditions like Alport syndrome and can rapidly detect mutations causing X-linked leucoencephalopathy, even when conventional methods fail. Furthermore, it facilitates high-throughput molecular diagnosis of diseases like von Willebrand disease by allowing for the analysis of numerous patients and relatives concurrently through single gene re-sequencing.

Shapiro syndrome commonly presents with recurrent hypothermia, hyperhidrosis, and agenesis of the corpus callosum, and may also include symptoms like vomiting or, in rare cases, cyclic bicytopenia and can occur in both childhood and later in life.
Several mouse models are used to study autism spectrum disorder, including BTBR T+ tf/J mice, Shank3-deficient mice, and MeCP2+/- mice.

BioASQ is a large-scale biomedical semantic indexing and question answering competition assessing systems’ ability to index articles and provide answers to natural language questions. It combines information from biomedical articles and ontologies to deliver concise and user-understandable responses. A publicly available evaluation infrastructure, including benchmark datasets, was developed through BIOASQ to evaluate systems assigning MESH headings or producing answers and summaries.

G3BP1 and G3BP2 contribute to stress granule formation, initiate stress granule assembly via multimerization, and play a role in cellular recovery from stresses like arsenite exposure, while also being required for the translation of interferon stimulated mRNAs, regulating the antiviral state, influencing mRNA expression levels, and potentially acting as negative regulators of p53 tumor suppressor pathway activity.

Epidural blood patch provides the cornerstone of invasive measures and is considered a treatment option for low pressure headache, though its effectiveness varies depending on the cause and severity of the headache.

A human genetic variant (Ser96Ala) in HRC has been linked to ventricular arrhythmia and sudden death in dilated cardiomyopathy. This variant diminishes the interaction of HRC with triadin, affecting ryanodine receptor stability and increasing SR calcium leak. Consequently, this leads to abnormal calcium cycling and cardiac arrhythmias, potentially inducing aftercontractions and delayed afterdepolarizations. HRC has also been linked with familiar cardiac conduction disease and malignant ventricular arrhythmias.

The incidence of sudden cardiac death among young athletes is estimated to be between 1 and 13.4 per 100,000 person years, though it may be underestimated and ranges from as low as 0.4 per 100,000 patient-years.

Amitriptyline is effective in reducing muscle inflammation and depressive symptoms in the mdx mouse model of Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Treatment with amitriptyline resulted in anxiolytic and antidepressant effects in these mice, which correlated with increased serotonin levels in the amygdala and hippocampus. Additionally, inflammation in the skeletal muscle tissue of mdx mice was reduced following amitriptyline treatment.

Histone H3 lysine 9 methylation, histone H3 trimethylation at lysine 36, histone H4 lysine 20 trimethylation, and histone H3 lysine 79 trimethylation are all associated with facultative heterochromatin, alongside histone H3 acetylation and di- and trimethylation of lysine 9 in certain contexts.
Mutations in the genes encoding TREX1, RNASEH2-A, RNASEH2-B, RNASEH2-C, and SAMHD1 have been associated with Aicardi-Goutieres syndrome.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Paenibacillus, Lysinibacillus, Klebsiella, Microbacterium, Chryseobacterium, and Geobacter species may be useful in uranium bioremediation, as these bacteria demonstrate uranium resistance, accumulation, binding capability, or dominance in communities actively involved in the process.

The QT interval in patients with short QT syndrome is less than 300-325 ms after correction for heart rate at rates below 80 beats per minute, though no definitive boundary for the QT interval has yet been determined.

Thyroid hormone is required for hypothalamic neurons that regulate cardiovascular functions, and disruptions to these neurons can result in hypertension. Specifically, a novel population of neurons in the anterior hypothalamus requires thyroid hormone receptor signaling for proper development, and their ablation leads to hypertension. Additionally, thyroid hormone resistance syndrome, caused by mutations in thyroid hormone receptors, impairs physiological responses to the hormone and is associated with the disease. Alterations in thyroid hormone levels also impact blood pressure, with hypothyroidism often linked to increased blood pressure and hyperthyroidism sometimes causing systolic hypertension.

Abiraterone is an androgen biosynthesis inhibitor that reduces androgen production by blocking the enzyme cytochrome P450 17 alpha-hydroxylase. It functions as a potent suppressor of cytochrome P450 17 α-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-mediated androgen production, effectively targeting both intratumoral androgens and the androgen receptor pathway. Additionally, abiraterone has recently demonstrated activity against AR and 3-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase.

Neprilysin can be delivered to the brain through several methods including fusion to ApoB for peripheral delivery across the blood-brain barrier via intra-peritoneal injection, fusion to albumin for sustained peripheral depletion via intravenous dosing, or by global brain delivery using an adeno-associated virus vector administered intracardiacally, and also through ex vivo gene delivery utilizing primary fibroblasts implanted into the brain.
Mitochondria originated from within the alpha-proteobacteria.
Mutations in BRAG1 have been identified in families with X-linked intellectual disability.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase (SERCA) is the membrane-bound pump that utilizes ATP to drive calcium ions from the myocyte cytosol against the higher calcium concentration in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, and is crucial for calcium homeostasis.
Hunter disease, or mucopolysaccharidosis type II, is an X-linked recessive disorder, with all other mucopolysaccharidoses being autosomal recessive except for Hunter syndrome.

Ephrins and their receptors play important roles in the development of the central nervous system and regulate cancer cell migration and invasion, particularly in glioblastoma, the most common brain cancer. Ephrin-A1 colocalizes with integrin α3 at the cell surface of glioblastoma cells, and ephrins bind to Eph receptors like EphA2 in these cells. Increased expression of EphA7 also correlates with adverse outcomes in glioblastoma patients, suggesting a role in disease progression.

The absence of Saccharomyces cerevisiae Rrm3p leads to replication fork pausing and breakage at numerous sites throughout the genome, activation of the intra-S phase checkpoint, slowed replication through telomeric and subtelomeric DNA, and increased fork stalling.

No, not all archaea possess multiple origins of DNA replication. While some archaea, including certain species of *Sulfolobus* and haloarchaea, replicate using multiple origins, others, like *Pyrococcus abyssi*, utilize a single origin, and some archaea, like eubacteria, seem to replicate using a single origin. It was previously thought archaea only had a single origin, but recent studies demonstrate multiple origins function in some species.

The ubiquitin proteome consists of thousands of ubiquitinated proteins and their ubiquitination sites, which are identified through high throughput approaches enabled by mass spectrometry following efficient purification methods.

RIP1 regulates major signaling pathways including necroptosis, apoptosis, and pro-survival/inflammation NF-κB activation, and is involved in signaling induced by TLRs as well as activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome through a RIP1-RIP3-DRP1 pathway.

The sympathetic nervous system dilates the pupil either directly on the dilator muscle or centrally by inhibiting the Edinger-Westphal nucleus, and its activation produces reflex dilation of the pupil in unanesthetized persons.

No, p105 is the precursor protein of the NF-kappaB subunit p50, and p100 is the precursor protein of p52. One document states that NF-kappaB1 (p105 and p50) regulates and functions as a transcription factor, while another details the processing of p105 into p50. Additionally, a separate document indicates that IKKalpha mediates processing of the NF-kappaB2 precursor protein p100, resulting in active production of the NF-kappaB subunit p52.

Epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) is a complex biological process where epithelial cells transform and gain characteristics of invasive mesenchymal cells. This process involves losing cell adhesion and polarity, rearranging cytoskeletons, and acquiring increased migratory capacity and invasiveness. EMT is essential for normal development but also contributes to pathologies like fibrosis and cancer progression, specifically playing a role in tumor growth and metastasis. It is a remarkably well-conserved mechanism governing morphogenesis in multicellular organisms.

Yes, gene fission and fusion rates differ depending on the lineage and organism. In rice, gene fission occurred at a higher rate than gene fusion. However, in Drosophila, fusion is more abundant than fission. Additionally, in prokaryotic two-component systems, fusion rates are affected by the presence of specific domains, suggesting an imbalance between the two processes.

Mutations in the Notch pathway have been implicated in T-cell lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma, adult T-cell leukemia, other cancers, Alagille syndrome, Multiple Sclerosis, CADASIL, Spondylocostal Dysostosis, and inherited genetic disorders.

Yes, cognitive behavioral therapy can improve fatigue in cancer patients. One study found physical training combined with cognitive behavioral therapy, and physical training alone, both had beneficial effects on fatigue compared to no intervention, though physical training was equally or more effective. Additionally, cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia in cancer patients may also improve fatigue. In adolescents with chronic fatigue syndrome, positive effects from cognitive behavioral therapy are sustained long-term.

Yes, Fbw7 is the substrate recognition component of an SCF-type ubiquitin ligase, a complex comprised of SKP1, CUL1, and an F-box protein. It mediates the degradation of various target proteins and is crucial for cell growth, survival, and differentiation.

Yes, urinary incontinence is a prevalent condition among female athletes, affecting between 41% and 49.5% depending on other factors. It is often under-reported, but the highest prevalence is reported in high-impact sports, and can impact performance and daily life.
Apert syndrome is typically a sporadic condition, but can also exhibit autosomal dominant inheritance with complete penetrance, and has been documented with both female and male transmission.

Experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis is the most widely used animal model for studying multiple sclerosis, as it exhibits similarities to the disease and is useful for understanding both immune-mediated pathology and the progressive clinical course.

Breast cancers in Li-Fraumeni syndrome are often hormone receptor and HER-2 positive, and most exhibit a triple-positive phenotype (estrogen receptor-positive/progesterone receptor-positive/HER2-positive).

Treatment options for triiodothyronine toxicosis include propranolol, though it is not supportive as sole therapy, and methimazole, with higher doses being more effective in childhood Graves’ disease, while monitoring free triiodothyronine and thyroid-stimulating hormone levels can help differentiate mild hyperthyroidism from delayed pituitary recovery.

Yes, enhancer RNAs (eRNAs) are a class of noncoding RNAs transcribed from enhancer regions. They are produced by the transcription of DNA enhancer fragments and participate in the regulation of coding gene transcription, as well as enhancer activation and enhancer-driven gene regulation. Studies indicate they are involved in various diseases, including cancers and neurodegenerative disorders.

The known human transmembrane nucleoporins are NDC1, POM121, gp210, and potentially additional membrane-integral nucleoporins beyond these, as vertebrate NPCs contain redundancies in protein-protein interactions for anchorage within the nuclear envelope.
The SCFbeta-Trcp and SCF(HOS) E3 ubiquitin ligases, as well as beta-TrCP2, mediate the ubiquitination and degradation of IFNAR1.

Yes, conserved noncoding elements are associated with the evolution of animal body plans. These elements cluster around genes that regulate development and can act as transcriptional enhancers, representing genomic traces of core gene regulatory networks that specify development of alternative body plans. Rewiring of gene regulatory networks containing key developmental coordinators was likely a driving force during this evolution, and different animal groups contain alternative sets of these elements.

RARβ(2), APC, SFRP1, XAF1, LOXL1, CDH1, FANCF, LOXL4, p16INK4, SOX9, TIG1, TIMP3, and XAF1 were found to be methylated in bladder cancer cells, with hepaCAM exon 2 also showing methylation and inhibiting hepaCAM expression.

Yes, DNA methylation appears to be a suitable marker for epigenetic phenotyping of tumors and can be used in NSCLC diagnosis. The stability and specificity of DNA methylation make it a valuable tool, and genomewide DNA methylation maps have been used to identify differentially methylated regions that can serve as candidate markers for diagnosis. Several studies have identified novel DMRs that show significant differences between tumor and control lung tissues, and even subtype-specific DMRs, suggesting diagnostic potential.
The prion-related domain of TIA-1 is necessary for stress granule assembly, as it mediates the assembly and its substitution with a yeast prion domain reconstitutes SG assembly.

Yes, the protein product of the cylindromatosis gene, CYLD, is a deubiquitinating enzyme. It is repeatedly described as such and shown to regulate various cellular processes through its deubiquitinating activity, with loss of this activity correlating with tumorigenesis and inflammatory responses.
The SET domain is a characteristic feature of most histone lysine methyltransferases, though some, like Dot1, do not contain this domain and instead methylate histone H3 lysine-79.

Single-molecule fluorescence in situ hybridization is a technique used for detecting RNAs within cells and tissues, providing information on gene expression. It is particularly useful for analyzing several transcripts simultaneously and can be combined with protein detection to understand the relationship between transcription, localization, and protein expression. This method allows for quantitative imaging of single RNA molecules and validation of RNA-sequencing analysis, revealing details about mRNA accumulation and targeting.

Several bioinformatics tools aid in gene structure prediction, including SpliceMachine, GeneSeqer with its associated SplicePredictor, PROSPECT-PSPP, and methods leveraging RNA structure information with tools like Vienna software alongside Markov models.
The major phytoalexin in alfalfa is the isoflavonoid (-)-medicarpin (or 6aR, 11aR)-medicarpin.

SPARC can inhibit the growth of several human cancers, including breast, pancreatic, melanoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma. In some instances, increased SPARC expression correlates with reduced tumor growth, decreased aggressiveness, and enhanced sensitivity to chemotherapy. Conversely, inhibiting SPARC expression can lead to increased cancer cell growth, migration, and angiogenesis, suggesting it often functions as a tumor suppressor. The effect of SPARC appears to be cell type-specific, with intracellular SPARC playing a role in melanoma cell proliferation.
Chediak-Higashi syndrome is associated with mutations in the LYST gene, as it occurs in humans, mink, and mice with defective versions of the gene.

Yes, delayed enhancement of the intraventricular septum was observed in a highly trained man after prolonged strenuous exercise involving running and cycling, as revealed by cardiac MRI. This finding may represent subtle inflammation secondary to combined exercise and altitude effects.

Dronedarone preferentially antagonizes thyroid hormone binding to thyroid hormone receptor alpha1. It does not alter the thyroid hormone profile in normal animals, though in thyroxine-treated animals, amiodarone—but not dronedarone—increases the T4/T3 ratio without changing T4, T3, or rT3 levels.  Furthermore, dronedarone administration can reduce resting heart rate and contractility, and suppress ischemic contracture. In hypothyroid hearts, dronedarone increases myocardial glycogen content and alters myosin heavy chain expression.

Selenoprotein synthesis in eukaryotes requires seryl-tRNA synthetase, phosphoseryl-tRNA(Sec) kinase, Sep-tRNA:Sec-tRNA synthase, selenophosphate synthase, the Sec-specific elongation factor EFSec, and SECIS elements located in the 3' untranslated regions of mRNAs, alongside protein factors recruited by these elements.

The ESCRT machinery is necessary for multivesicular body formation and the degradation of transmembrane proteins in lysosomes, as well as midbody abscission during cytokinesis and retroviral budding. It controls membrane dynamics and is involved in sorting ubiquitinated transmembrane proteins into vesicles. Furthermore, the ESCRT machinery plays a role in virion assembly and release, specifically mediating fission between a nascent virus and the plasma membrane.

p15, p16, p18, p19, and p27 have been found to inhibit cyclin dependent kinase 4, with p15 and p27 demonstrating coordinated inhibitory actions and p18 specifically binding to and inhibiting CDK4 and CDK6.

Thyrotropin-releasing hormone plays an important role in central cardiovascular regulation and is involved in the development or maintenance of hypertension. Overexpression of TRH induces hypertension, while its inhibition via antisense treatment reduces arterial blood pressure in hypertensive rats. Central TRH hyperactivity is present in spontaneously hypertensive rats, and it participates in hypertension induced by body weight gain, likely through its action on sympathetic activity.

Yes, triadin is a critical determinant of cellular calcium cycling and contractility in the heart and is involved in the regulation of cardiac excitation-contraction coupling. It forms a complex with other proteins to regulate calcium release and is essential for normal cardiac calcium handling and function; alterations in its expression or interaction with other proteins lead to impaired relaxation, depressed contractility, and cardiac hypertrophy.

Mutations in the HAMP gene are associated with juvenile hemochromatosis, iron overload, and iron-refractory anemia, while abnormal hepcidin expression also impacts the promotion and progression of cancer including hepatoma.
Inhibitor-1 is the main protein inhibitor of protein phosphatase 1 (PP1).

Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are involved in the development and progression of systemic lupus erythematosus. They can serve as a source of autoantigens, potentially driving the autoimmune response, and contribute to inflammation by activating the NLRP3 inflammasome and releasing inflammatory cytokines. Aberrant NETosis and impaired NET clearance correlate with disease activity and can damage tissues like the endothelium, contributing to conditions such as lupus nephritis and accelerated atherosclerosis.
Heat-shock protein 90 is required for efficient targeting of Argonaute 2 to P-bodies and stress granules.

No. The provided information details the approval of ustekinumab, RSVPreF, nirsevimab, hydroxyurea, axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel, and darolutamide by the United States Food and Drug Administration, but does not mention Mammaprint.

Von Hippel-Lindau disease is an inherited cancer syndrome that can affect multiple systems of the body, including the retina and central nervous system. It is caused by mutations of the von Hippel-Lindau tumor suppressor gene and can predispose individuals to various tumors like hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma. The disease exhibits variable expression, with clinical symptoms typically appearing after the age of 30, and can also be associated with conditions like acute myeloid leukemia.
Yes, the context indicates a HER2 S310F mutation is an *active* mutation located in the extracellular *dimerization* domain of HER2, suggesting activity is linked to dimerization.
A pharmacogenetic test identifying the HLA-B*5701 allele is available for screening prior to abacavir prescription to reduce the incidence of hypersensitivity reactions.

Yes, microRNAs can be regulated through DNA methylation of their promoters. Several instances demonstrate that aberrant DNA methylation of microRNA gene promoters leads to gene silencing, impacting carcinogenesis and disease states like colorectal cancer, lung cancer, lupus, and multiple myeloma. Specifically, methylation of promoter regions correlates with decreased microRNA expression, and inhibiting DNA methylation can restore microRNA expression.

Yes, an asymmetric pattern of positioned nucleosomes flanks the ARS consensus sequence at yeast origins, and local nucleosomes are an important determinant for origin selection and function. Replication origins are also characterized by rigid DNA, similar to promoters, which may facilitate protein binding like the origin recognition complex.

In higher eukaryote genomes, TA and CG dinucleotides, GCA.TGC and CTA.TAG trinucleotides tend to be under-represented, and several four-base-pair palindromes and the tetranucleotide CTAG are also rare.

Yes, nimotuzumab may improve survival in glioblastoma patients. Several sources indicate improved progression-free survival or overall survival with nimotuzumab treatment, either alone or in combination with radiotherapy and/or temozolomide, in newly diagnosed or recurrent cases of glioblastoma. One study in Chinese patients showed median overall survival of 15.9 months with this combination, while another suggests it might improve survival time. Additionally, clinical experience demonstrates increased survival with nimotuzumab use in pediatric patients with glioblastoma.
Stiff-man syndrome may occur with dementia, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, breast cancer, Graves’ disease, epilepsy, and other organ-specific autoimmune diseases.

Adaptive mutagenesis in Bacillus subtilis involves multiple mechanisms, including those regulated by genes comA and comK, and is influenced by transcription and transcription-associated proteins like Mfd. The mismatch DNA repair system also plays a role, with deficiencies in MutSL increasing mutagenesis, and overexpression of mutS decreasing it. Furthermore, proteins YqjH and YqjW, homologs of DNA polymerases involved in mutagenesis, contribute to this process, particularly YqjH in generating revertants.

The Ten-Item Personality Inventory can evaluate openness, extroversion, conscientiousness, agreeableness, and emotional stability, as well as facets like anxiety, assertiveness, activity, vulnerability, and ideas.

The control of tubulin acetylation involves the acetyltransferase MEC-17 and the deacetylases SIRT2, HDAC6, and α-tubulin acetyltransferase 1 (ATAT1), which oppose each other to govern acetylation levels.

Yes, ANP increases the transcapillary escape rate of albumin in both normal subjects and those with diabetes. It is noted to increase albumin escape during infusion compared to control, and in diabetics, the transcapillary escape rate of albumin, already elevated at baseline, further increased with ANP infusion.

Glivec, also known as Imatinib, has approved uses in treating chronic myeloid leukemia, inoperable gastrointestinal stromal tumors when the tumor overexpresses c-KIT, and saw an increase in prescriptions for approved uses over off-label uses.

APOBEC3G and APOBEC3F, as well as SAMHD1, induce inhibition of LINE-1 and Alu retrotransposition, with APOBEC3G being a major contributor to this inhibitory activity and its effectiveness linked to oligomerization and binding to 7SL RNA, while SAMHD1 regulates retrotransposition by reducing ORF2p levels.

No, while telomere length decreases with age, there is substantial variation between individuals, making accurate age prediction unreliable. One study found the correlation between telomere length and age too low for forensic use, and even after accounting for factors like sex and ethnicity, prediction accuracies remained low.
Standardized mortality rates from cardiovascular diseases and all cancers are lower in individuals with G6PD deficiency compared to those with the wild-type G6PD.

Macrostomum lignano is a versatile model organism for investigating ion channels and stem cells because bioelectrical signals generated by these channels are crucial in many cellular processes. This flatworm is useful for studying how membrane potential influences stem cell maintenance, proliferation, and differentiation, though the specific mechanisms are not yet fully understood. Researchers can manipulate membrane potential in *M. lignano* to observe effects on regeneration, and experimental tools for this animal model have recently been developed.

Yes, elevated C-reactive protein levels are associated with worse outcomes in subarachnoid hemorrhage patients. Studies suggest a relationship between higher CRP concentrations and the development of cerebral vasospasm and delayed ischemic neurologic deficits, both potentially devastating complications, and increasing severity of the initial ictus correlates with overall CRP values.
Benserazide is usually co-administered with L-DOPA as a peripheral aromatic amino acid decarboxylase inhibitor to treat Parkinson’s disease.
Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia caused by mutations in the ryanodine receptor RYR2 gene exhibits an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and explains nearly 70% of CPVT cases.

The INSURE procedure, or Intubation-SURfactant-Extubation, involves surfactant therapy during brief intubation immediately followed by extubation and continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) treatment. It is used for very low birthweight infants with respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) and has shown success in improving lung function and reducing the need for respiratory support. The procedure did not disrupt cerebral oxygen delivery, but it was associated with decreased electrical brain activity for an extended period.

Biomedical text mining tools for detecting protein-protein interactions range from simple keyword-based algorithms and tools coupled with biomedical named entity taggers to more complex systems utilizing advanced computational linguistic techniques, such as the OntoGene system which achieved best-ranked results in the BioCreative II.5 challenge, though current tools are often complex to install, require reimplementation, or do not achieve state-of-the-art performance.

Thyrocyte destruction in autoimmune thyroiditis can occur through inappropriate expression of Fas or TRAIL death pathway molecules and downregulation of the apoptosis controlling protein Bcl-2, often induced by cytokines released from infiltrating lymphocytes. Interactions between Fas and its ligand, as well as CD95 and CD95L, mediate thyrocyte destruction, and this process is influenced by the balance of T helper 1 and T helper 2 cytokines. Specifically, IL-1beta induces Fas expression on thyrocytes, leading to apoptosis when Fas is crosslinked, and autocrine/paracrine Fas-Fas ligand interaction contributes to thyrocyte cell death.

A-type lamins are thought to serve as a site for heterochromatin maintenance and transcriptional silencing, but are also required for the reduction of heterochromatin and downregulation of heterochromatin during gene activation.
Light-harvesting complex II is the most abundant membrane protein on Earth.

Yes, RNA sequencing can be used for the analysis of nascent transcripts. Cellular fractionation enriches the nuclear fraction for unprocessed RNA, making it suitable for analyzing nascent transcripts and RNA processing dynamics. Additionally, a model system for co-transcriptional splicing in vitro was established, indicating that RNA sequencing can detect transcripts still attached to RNA polymerase II.

Yes, RNA:DNA hybrids preferentially form in GC-rich regions. Evidence indicates these hybrids are preferentially formed at GC-rich trinucleotide and tetranucleotide repeat sequences, and genes with terminal GC skew also form R-loops. Additionally, ciRNAs with high GC% tend to form DNA:RNA hybrids.

Aberrant G-quadruplex formation is linked to genome instability and cancer, and myocardial fibrosis—a key pathological change contributing to heart failure, arrhythmias, and sudden death—is also associated with these structures.

Yes, shadow enhancers are associated with development, as they regulate gene expression during processes like gastrulation, heart development, and larval trichome patterning. They contribute to establishing positional identity and fostering robustness in developmental processes, and can modulate the expression of genes critical for these developments.

Yes, greater adherence to a prudent diet may reduce the risk of cardiovascular mortality and disease, and is considered cardioprotective. It is associated with a 28% lower risk of cardiovascular mortality and a 22% higher risk of cardiovascular disease is associated with a Western pattern. Additionally, the American Heart Association strongly recommends the prudent diet for all healthy adolescents to affect risk factor status for cardiovascular disease.

Yes, a hypothesis suggests Crohn’s disease may be provoked by exposure to micro-organisms surviving refrigerator temperatures, and studies indicate earlier exposure to refrigerators and freezers is associated with an increased risk of developing the disease. One study found refrigerator exposure at birth was a risk factor for CD.
TAS-102 is comprised of two active drugs: trifluridine, a nucleoside analog, and tipiracil hydrochloride, a thymidine phosphorylase inhibitor.

Triple negative breast cancer is characterized by the lack of expression of oestrogen and progesterone receptors and the human epidermal growth factor receptor type 2, resulting in a lack of established markers and rationale-based therapies.

Erucism is a skin reaction caused by envenomation from certain poisonous caterpillar bristles, or by urtication from Lepidoptera larvae. It can occur through contact with the skin or, in some cases, through ingestion. Specific caterpillars like *Lonomia* and *Thaumetopoea wilkinsoni* are known to cause erucism, and a unique form called Pararama is caused by *Premolis* larvae.

Candida glabrata is generally resistant to histatin 5, unlike Candida albicans, and requires higher concentrations for any fungicidal effect. This resistance is not due to differences in cell surface or biofilm matrix components, but rather a lack of functional polyamine transporters Dur3p and Dur31p, which are necessary for Hst5 to enter the cell. Introducing genes for these transporters into C. glabrata increases Hst5 uptake and killing, reversing the resistance. While C. glabrata biofilms exhibit some susceptibility to histatin 5, they are significantly less sensitive than C. albicans biofilms.
Galactocerebrosidase is deficient in Krabbe disease, leading to the accumulation of its cytotoxic substrate, psychosine.

No. The provided information discusses conserved noncoding elements in murine, metazoan, vertebrate, fly, and nematode genomes, specifically relating to animal development and body plans. There is no mention of CNEs being present or studied in plant genomes.

RIFIN proteins are clonally variant antigens expressed by the malaria parasite *Plasmodium falciparum*. Some RIFIN variants are associated with the parasite and transported to the surface of infected erythrocytes via Maurer's clefts, while others are mostly retained inside the parasite. RIFINs are also expressed in merozoites, appearing in the apical region or cytosol, indicating antigenic variation is not limited to the infected erythrocyte surface.

Current treatments for generalised anxiety disorder include manualised cognitive-behavioural therapy, such as the FRIENDS program, pharmacological treatment with selective serotonin re-uptake inhibitors like sertraline or paroxetine, and potentially, web-based e-health applications like E-couch, though evidence for the latter’s effectiveness in adults with the disorder is still emerging.

Yes, tetracycline can affect tooth formation. Several instances detail how tetracycline administered during tooth formation causes discoloration, enamel defects, and is incorporated into developing teeth, with the extent of the effect related to dosage and duration of therapy. It is specifically noted that administering tetracycline to children or pregnant women is known to adversely affect the color of developing teeth.

Several SNPs in genes including MC1R, ASIP, MATP, SLC24A5, TYR, TYRP1, and OCA2, as well as variants like C451T and C478T within the MC1R gene, are genetic determinants of hair pigmentation that may be useful in forensic analyses for predicting hair color and potentially skin type.

Correct classification of patients with aplastic anemia is mandatory for appropriate management, as adult hematologists should consider adult presentations of inherited disorders like Fanconi anemia to avoid incorrect treatment. A high index of suspicion and specific testing should be employed in both children and adults presenting with aplastic anemia to identify potential inherited disorders. Genetic counseling is important for families with Fanconi anemia, particularly those belonging to complementation group B due to its X-linked inheritance. Ultimately, understanding the role of Fanconi anemia proteins in DNA repair and replication stress is crucial for managing this genetically heterogeneous disorder and its associated risks of cancer and bone marrow failure.

Yes, CPVT is an important cause of cardiac syncope and sudden death, particularly in childhood, and can lead to ventricular fibrillation syncope or sudden cardiac death in young cases with normal structural hearts. It is also a potential cause of sudden cardiac death in athletes under 35 years of age.

Yes, ApoE exhibits anti-inflammatory properties and mimetics of ApoE can mimic this activity. Several instances describe ApoE as having anti-inflammatory effects in the central nervous system and its mimetics reducing inflammatory states, cytokines, and inflammatory infiltrates.

Icatibant and ecallantide, medications developed to control hereditary angioedema—a disorder also linked to kallikrein-kinin activation—have shown some success in treating ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema, with icatibant demonstrating rapid symptom relief and a shortened attack duration.

Yes, ACE inhibitors may decrease the incidence of radiation pneumonitis in patients receiving thoracic radiation for lung cancer and have demonstrated anticancer activity in lung cells, potentially reducing tumor growth and metastasis. Additionally, they are being investigated for mitigating radiation-induced lung injury in these patients.

The Tpl2 gene is associated with prostate cancer, squamous cell carcinoma of the skin, colitis-associated cancer, and T-cell neoplasias, specifically Large Granular Lymphocyte proliferative disorders.

Hydrochlorothiazide is indicated for the treatment of hypertension, particularly in higher age and isolated systolic hypertension, and is frequently used in combination with renin-angiotensin system blockers, ACE inhibitors and amiloride, as well as for treating swellings and shortness of breath associated with heart failure, and can be used in hypertensive disorders in pregnancy, though its use may result in a greater increase in carotid artery wall thickness compared to isradipine.

No, the majority of bacterial genes are located on the leading strand, and there is variation in the percentage of genes on each strand across different bacteria. Several factors influence this distribution, including collisions between replication and transcription machinery, selection for uninterrupted gene expression, and balancing forces that prevent all genes from moving to the leading strand. There is evidence suggesting a greater transfer of genes from the lagging strand to the leading strand than vice versa, indicating an asymmetry.

Patients with Apert syndrome present with shortened, deviated phalanges, extensive skin deficits following syndactyly release, and complex syndactyly requiring osteotomies, as well as notable abnormalities in vascular anatomy and, in some cases, polydactyly or ectrodactyly.

Yes, c-Myc is under circadian regulation, as it is rhythmically expressed and its mRNA and protein levels oscillate. Furthermore, BMAL1 inactivation disrupts the circadian expression pattern of c-Myc, indicating a relationship between the circadian clock and c-Myc expression.
Velcade (bortezomib) is mainly used to treat multiple myeloma.

Several genes are thought to be involved in medulloblastoma development, including PTCH1, SUFU, PTEN, CREBBP, MYT1L, Nfia, Nfib, Tead1, Tgif2, BMI1, and PCGF2, with alterations in SHH pathway genes and epigenetic modifications involving histone deacetylases and Polycomb group genes also playing a role.

The prevalence of short QT syndrome varies across studied populations, ranging from 0.03% in a Japanese population to 1.25% among males and 1.63% among females in a hospital-based consultation group, though estimates based on a cutoff of <300ms suggest it may be underdiagnosed, particularly in children.

Commotio cordis has been associated with risks in baseball, softball, football, basketball, hockey, lacrosse, soccer, cricket, and general daily or recreational activities, with lacrosse and hockey showing a higher frequency of deaths and soccer being particularly common internationally.

Yes, ultrasound lung comets increase after breath-hold diving, which involves intense physical exertion and altered breathing patterns, and can also increase after fish-catching diving competitions. These comets are a sign of extra-vascular lung water accumulation, and increases were observed after dynamic surface apnea, deep breath-hold sessions, and static maximal apnea. Additionally, asymptomatic SCUBA dives are associated with accumulation of extravascular lung water.

Hot water epilepsy is a rare form of reflex epilepsy triggered by bathing with hot water, and it typically occurs in children. Seizures are provoked by both cutaneous and heat stimuli, and can manifest as brief seizures with activity arrest, hypotonia, and sometimes clonic movements. The condition generally has a favorable prognosis, often resolving by simply decreasing the temperature of the bathwater, though antiepileptic drugs can be used if needed; psychomotor development remains normal after seizures. While primarily described in India and Japan, it can occur in other regions and is sometimes confused with other conditions like febrile convulsions.

Breath test biomarkers of pulmonary tuberculosis include derivatives of naphthalene, benzene, and alkanes, as well as cyclohexane and alkane derivatives indicative of oxidative stress, and specifically methyl phenylacetate, methyl p-anisate, methyl nicotinate, and o-phenylanisole, though methyl salicylate and methyl nicotinate may cause false positives due to their presence in other sources.

2-hydroxy-dATP induces A:T-->G:C, A:T-->C:G, and G:C-->T:A mutations, as well as G x C to A x T transitions and G x C to T x A transversions, and can also elicit substitution and deletion mutations including a G.C-->A.T transition—specifically a tandem (CC-->TT) mutation—though it enhances A.T --> C.G transversions when coexisting with 8-hydroxy-2'-deoxyguanosine 5'-triphosphate.

Afamelanotide is undergoing trials for the treatment of vitiligo, erythropoietic protoporphyria, polymorphic light eruption, and can potentially prevent actinic keratoses in organ transplant recipients, while also showing promise as a therapeutic tool in other light-related skin diseases and potentially other medical conditions.

Agrobacterium tumefaciens induces tumors on plants by transferring a nucleoprotein complex, the T-complex, into plant cells. This T-complex consists of T-DNA, the VirD2 endonuclease, and potentially VirE2. VirD2 interacts with plant host cyclophilins, and this interaction is important for T-DNA transfer, ultimately leading to tumor formation.

Yes, cffDNA enables reliable, timely, safe, and cost-effective diagnosis and testing without invasive methods, and new non-invasive prenatal tests utilizing cffDNA have been developed for fetal aneuploidy and potentially single gene disorders. These tests are being considered for integration into standard prenatal care and can be used to determine fetal sex with a high level of accuracy.

Perry syndrome features include autosomal dominant Parkinsonism, depression, severe weight loss, hypoventilation, respiratory failure, autonomic dysfunction such as orthostatic hypotension, decreased cardiac uptake, vertical gaze palsy, progressive midbrain atrophy, distinctive craniocervical levodopa induced dyskinesias, a good response to high-dose levodopa therapy, frontal cognitive impairment, oculogyric crisis, supranuclear gaze palsy, and marked loss of dopamine transporter binding with only mild parkinsonism.
Calsequestrin is the main calcium buffer and binding protein in the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Yes, patients with the UGT1A1*28 polymorphism have a higher risk of severe neutropenia and diarrhea, and screening for this polymorphism may identify patients with altered irinotecan pharmacokinetics. It is noted that the frequency of UGT1A1*28 is high in Caucasians.
Members of the 2-aminobenzamide class of histone deacetylase inhibitors show promise as therapeutics for neurodegenerative diseases such as Friedreich's ataxia and Huntington's disease.

Yes, SMN interacts with at least two of the U6-associated Sm-like proteins, Lsm4 and Lsm6, and the carboxyl-terminal arginine- and glycine-rich domain of Lsm4 directly interacts with SMN. Additionally, symmetrical dimethylation of arginine residues in LSm4 decisively influences its binding to the SMN Tudor domain.

Several anti-amyloid-beta monoclonal antibodies investigated in clinical trials for Alzheimer’s disease treatment include aducanumab, donanemab, gantenerumab, lecanemab, solanezumab, bapineuzumab, crenezumab, and trontinemab.

No, MVIIA and MVIIC bind to distinct calcium channels. MVIIA recognizes N-type calcium channels, while MVIIC binds to a calcium channel alpha 1 subunit distinct from the target of MVIIA, and primarily blocks P/Q-type calcium channels with high affinity.

The fractal globule is a compact polymer state that arises during polymer condensation due to topological constraints preventing chain regions from crossing over one another. It’s a long-lived intermediate state recently proposed as a structural model for human chromosomes, potentially up to 10 Mb in scale. This conformation allows for dense packing of the genome while still enabling genomic loci to fold and unfold easily, distinguishing it from a standard globular equilibrium model.

Etoposide, doxorubicin, mitoxantrone, m-amsacrine, teniposide, and dovitinib are anticancer drugs that target human topoisomerase II, with some inducing cell death through apoptosis or increasing levels of covalent enzyme-cleaved DNA complexes.

SHAPE-Seq is a high-throughput technique used to measure quantitative, single nucleotide-resolution secondary and tertiary structural information for many RNA molecules simultaneously. It combines selective 2'-hydroxyl acylation analyzed by primer extension (SHAPE) chemistry with multiplexed paired-end deep sequencing. This method achieves structural characterization of RNAs and provides reactivity data that can be used to predict RNA structures, and a newer version, SHAPE-Seq v2.0, can obtain reactivity information for every nucleotide without needing a specific priming site.

Inhibitors of TPL2/MAP3K8 include EPAC inhibitors and TPL2 inhibitors, which were found to reduce β-AR agonist-induced IL-1β reporter activity and NA-induced endogenous Il1b mRNA expression and IL-1β protein production, as well as methods to suppress TPL2 expression and its kinase activity which inhibited cell proliferation.
The mode of inheritance for Fanconi anemia is autosomal recessive, except for complementation group B, which is X-linked.

Conserved noncoding sequences (CNS) are found within and outside Hox gene clusters and appear to play a critical role in regulating Hox gene expression, contributing to coordinated temporal and spatial patterns during embryonic development. These sequences represent putative cis-regulatory elements and are often conserved across diverse chordates, including humans, mouse, fugu, elephant shark, and amphioxus.  Comparisons across species have identified a large number of these conserved noncoding elements, some of which overlap with known enhancers and regulatory protein binding sites, suggesting their functional importance in gene regulation. Even in species with extensive genome duplication, like fugu, ancient CNS are often maintained, though some have diverged, and their presence correlates with Hox expression patterns.

Oligopaint probes are fluorescently labeled, single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides used to visualize genomic regions ranging from tens of kilobases to many megabases. These probes can be synthesized using molecular biological techniques and utilized in fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), including 3D-FISH, for visualizing chromosomes in cell culture, fixed tissues, and metaphase spreads. Combining Oligopaint technology with techniques like Stochastic Optical Reconstruction Microscopy (STORM) and DNA-PAINT enables single-molecule super-resolution imaging of nucleic acids with resolution down to 20nm. Researchers have precise control over targeted sequences, allowing for single and multicolor imaging of genomic regions.

Targeted Chromatin Capture (T2C) is a novel method used to detect genomic interactions and regulatory elements within selected regions of the genome. It provides an unbiased view of spatial organization at a high resolution—ranging from 2 to 6 kbp—and at a lower cost than methods like Hi-C due to reduced sequencing effort. T2C is an efficient and affordable tool for addressing genome compartmentalization and chromatin-interaction networks, suitable for both clinical and non-clinical research.

Reported adverse effects of topical minoxidil include mild scalp dryness and irritation, and rarely, allergic contact dermatitis. Some individuals may experience smoking intolerance, and central serous chorioretinopathy has been observed with long-term use. While deaths occurred during clinical studies, these were largely attributed to pre-existing cardiovascular abnormalities or other underlying conditions, and significant adverse effects are unlikely due to low systemic absorption.

Yes, arimoclomol is described as a co-inducer of heat shock protein expression and a co-inducer of the heat shock stress response in multiple instances, suggesting it stimulates the heat shock response. It is also referred to as a heat-shock protein co-inducer.
Memory problems, including loss of memory and problems with attention, memory, and reasoning, are reported in Gulf War Syndrome.

Heyde syndrome involves the coincidence of aortic valve stenosis and gastrointestinal bleeding, often originating from angiodysplasia. It is sometimes associated with acquired coagulopathy and anemia due to intestinal blood loss.
