Several circular RNA prediction tools are available, including PcircRNA_finder, find_circ, CIRCexplorer, tandem, DCC, and CircTest DCC, alongside algorithms classified as pseudo-reference-based and split-alignment-based approaches.
Nusinersen treats spinal muscular atrophy, a rare recessive disorder characterized by muscle atrophy and weakness resulting from motor neuron degeneration.
The London mutation is associated with early-onset Alzheimer's disease and is a cause of Alzheimer’s disease.

Long noncoding RNAs are a novel class of transcripts similar to mRNA but without known protein-coding potential. These RNAs demonstrate a wide range of structural and functional roles in cellular biology and are an integral functional component of the mammalian transcriptome. They are often dysregulated in tumors and contribute to various biological phenomena, including antiviral responses and disease pathogenesis. Recent research has shown extensive, tissue-specific expression of these RNAs in both normal tissues and cancers.
The human gut is home to approximately 10<sup>13-14</sup> microorganisms, with a total of around 10<sup>14</sup> cells and approximately 500 species per individual.

3,4-diaminobenzoic acid derivatives can downregulate macrophage activation and cross-presentation by dendritic cells, potentially leading to the targeted downregulation of immune responses and possessing immune-regulating properties by inhibiting the oxytocinase subfamily of M1 aminopeptidases.

Yes, apocynin and diphenylene iodonium are both identified as NADPH oxidase inhibitors in multiple instances, including in isolated pulmonary arteries, microglial cells responding to oAβ, and in response to paraquat-induced toxicity, as well as in regulating microglia proliferation.
Viliuisk encephalomyelitis is diagnosed among the Iakut people of Siberia, specifically originating in the Middle Viliui region and spreading throughout the Yakut Republic of the Russian Federation.

Septins are a family of conserved GTP-binding proteins found in eukaryotic cells, ranging from yeasts to humans. They form filaments and higher-order structures that associate with actin and microtubule networks, coordinating cell division, maintaining cell polarity, and remodeling membranes. These proteins participate in diverse cellular functions including cytokinesis, organization of actin networks, vesicle trafficking, and cell surface organization, and are essential for this process in both fungal and animal cells. Misregulation of septins has been observed in neurodegenerative diseases and cancers.
Simtuzumab targets sLOXL2, as studies evaluated its effect on patients with varying concentrations of this molecule to assess progression-free survival.

Dravet syndrome is a severe infantile-onset epileptic encephalopathy often beginning with fever-triggered hemiclonic or generalized clonic seizures in the first year of life, frequently leading to intellectual impairment, and associated with mutations in the sodium channel genes SCN1A and SCN1B.

Telomerase is a good drug target because it is upregulated in a high percentage of cancer cells—ranging from 80% to nearly 100% in some cancers like adenocarcinoma and breast cancer—but is largely absent in healthy tissue. This difference in expression makes it a potentially selective target for anti-cancer therapies. Additionally, targeting telomerase may also reduce drug resistance and, in cancers with short telomeres, cells become vulnerable to telomerase inhibition.

DENdb is a centralized, on-line repository of predicted enhancers derived from multiple human cell-lines. It integrates enhancers predicted by five different methods to create a comprehensive catalogue of potential enhancers. The database facilitates searching, browsing, and visualization of information related to enhancer properties, interactions, and neighboring genes. It also provides information about overlap with DNase I hypersensitive regions and transcription factor binding motifs.

The Tokuhashi Scoring System, revised Tokuhashi score, and Tomita score are used for scoring patients with spinal metastasis, with modified versions of the Tokuhashi and Tomita scores also being utilized to predict patient survival and determine treatment strategies.
No. The provided information details regulatory subunits required for Nox1, Nox2, Nox3, and Nox4, but does not mention any requirement for Nox5 to function with other subunits.

Side effects during statin administration in patients with atherosclerosis include myopathies, rhabdomyolysis, an increased risk of diabetes mellitus, and potentially heart failure/pulmonary edema, particularly in elderly patients presenting with acute coronary syndrome, while rare cases may involve an autoimmune myopathy with anti-HMGCR antibodies.
Entresto is a combination of sacubitril and valsartan, functioning as both an angiotensin receptor neprilysin inhibitor and an angiotensin receptor blocker.

doRiNA is a database of RNA interactions in post-transcriptional regulation, systematically curating and integrating binding site data for RNA binding proteins (RBPs) and microRNAs (miRNAs). It supports research aimed at deciphering the post-transcriptional regulatory code formed by the combined action of RBPs and miRNAs on mRNA. The database offers a user interface, search functions, and is accessible via REST and Python APIs, all under an Open Source license.

Yes, NSD-1015 is described as an aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase inhibitor in multiple instances, and it is noted to inhibit AADC activity in both the liver and striatum of rats. It is also referred to as a central aromatic amino acid dopa decarboxylase inhibitor used to study the actions of L-DOPA.

Yes, pseudouridine is the most abundant of over 100 chemically distinct natural ribonucleotide modifications in RNA and is considered a post-transcriptional modification of RNA. It is also shown to ablate activity when incorporated into RNA, suggesting it alters the RNA’s natural state.

Human ABCC gene products transport a structurally diverse array of important endogenous substances and xenobiotics and their metabolites, often conferring resistance to anticancer drugs and being subject to inhibition by various compounds.

Furin is essential for the processing of hepcidin, converting pro-hepcidin into its mature, bioactive 25-amino acid form. Pro-hepcidin lacks biological activity unless furin processes it, and inhibiting furin or reducing its expression abolishes hepcidin’s ability to degrade ferroportin. Several convertases including furin, PACE4, PC5, and PC7 are involved in the generation and secretion of active hepcidin, and furin mediates posttranslational processing of hepcidin in human hepatocytes.

CIDEC protein is expressed in white adipose tissues and is localized to lipid droplets. It is found in human adipocytes, where it regulates lipolysis and promotes fat droplet formation. Furthermore, CIDEC expression in human white adipose tissue correlates positively with insulin sensitivity.

Moderate consumption of filtered coffee is weakly inversely associated with stroke risk, although clear biological mechanisms for cardiovascular benefits are not yet known, and coffee consumption may lower energy and dietary fat intake depending on an individual’s CYP1A2 rs762551 genotype.

The Orpington Prognostic Scale is used to predict functional outcome after a stroke, including activities of daily living and physical function. It helps estimate a patient’s future functional status and can indicate response to rehabilitation, particularly in elderly patients. Additionally, the scale provides information regarding admission into Geriatric Units of Medium Stay when considered alongside a Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment.

Yes, intensive transcription at one locus, such as immediate-early genes (IEGs), frequently spills over into neighboring loci, resulting in co-upregulation of neighboring genes and activation in surrounding intergenic regions. This suggests transcriptional activation has a ripple effect that may be advantageous for coordinated expression. Changes in IEG expression also indicate activity in cortical circuits.
methylKit is an R package that rapidly analyzes genome-wide cytosine epigenetic profiles from high-throughput methylation and hydroxymethylation sequencing experiments.

Yes, T cells regulate neuropathic pain. Evidence indicates T cell infiltration occurs after nerve injury and their depletion or modulation impacts pain sensitivity; specifically, type 1 T cells enhance pain while type 2 T cells attenuate it, and regulatory T cells may limit inflammation and reduce pain levels. Additionally, interactions between macrophages and T cells via the GITRL-GITR pathway contribute to neuropathic pain.
The combined incidence of X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy and its variants in Australasia is estimated to be at least 1.6 per 100,000 individuals.

Yes, telomerase gene therapy rescues telomere length in mice with aplastic anemia, improving survival. Additionally, telomerase enables tumor cells to maintain telomere length, allowing indefinite replicative capacity, and activation of telomerase can increase telomere length in cells.

Yes, ABCE1 is involved in ribosomal recycling. Several sources describe ABCE1 as a ribosome-recycling factor essential for translation termination and re-initiation, orchestrating a process where it tears ribosome subunits apart and connects translation termination with mRNA surveillance. It is also crucial for ribosome recycling *in vivo* and controls ribosome homeostasis.

Clinical features of de Morsier syndrome include optic nerve hypoplasia, absence of the septum pellucidum, hypopituitarism, decreased visual acuity, neurodevelopmental disabilities, poor vision, nystagmus, bitemporal hemianopia, hypothalamic dysfunction, developmental delay, and potentially autism or diabetes insipidus.

Yes, several instances suggest oculocutaneous albinism can be inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern. One description details a sister and brother with the condition, with autosomal recessive inheritance being possible. Further analysis of brown oculocutaneous albinism in families and a specific case family also demonstrated this inheritance pattern, and another instance notes pedigrees consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance.
The DAL cluster is the largest metabolic gene cluster in yeast, consisting of six adjacent genes that enable Saccharomyces cerevisiae to use allantoin as a nitrogen source.

The MCAST algorithm identifies candidate cis-regulatory modules (CRMs) consisting of clusters of transcription factor binding motifs within a genomic sequence. It can search long DNA sequences and model length distributions between motifs within a regulatory module. Furthermore, MCAST can predict CRMs while incorporating epigenomic data and provides statistical confidence estimates based on false discovery rate estimation.
ApoE epsilon4 homozygosity is associated with both atherosclerosis and Alzheimer’s disease.

No, ultraconserved elements are strongly depleted among segmental duplications and copy number variants. This depletion is observed across multiple datasets, including newly formed copy number variations and in genomes of both mammals and plants.
Bacillus anthracis causes woolsorter's disease, which is a form of pneumonic anthrax.
RADAR, a rigorously annotated database of A-to-I RNA editing, is available at http://RNAedit.com.

No, normal human somatic cells do not or only transiently express telomerase, which includes the TERT protein. This limited expression leads to telomere shortening with each cell division, contrasting with cancer cells that typically express high levels of telomerase and exhibit unlimited cell proliferation.

In budding yeast, Cse4, H4, H2A, H2B, and histone H3, as well as the CEN-C and CEN-T proteins in *Neurospora crassa*, recognize centromeric DNA, and HP1 complexes also interact with centromeric histones to direct DNA methylation and deacetylation.

Yes, Gomafu is acutely regulated in response to neuronal activation and involved in schizophrenia-associated alternative splicing, and its dysregulation leads to alternative splicing patterns observed in schizophrenia for genes like DISC1 and ERBB4. Furthermore, Gomafu is downregulated in post-mortem cortical gray matter from individuals with schizophrenia, and genetic variants in the related long non-coding RNA MIAT contribute to the risk of paranoid schizophrenia.

Yes, butterfly rash is a symptom of Systemic lupus erythematosus. Several instances describe a butterfly rash in connection with a diagnosis or potential diagnosis of SLE, and one report details successful treatment of facial lesions *of* SLE that included a butterfly rash. Additionally, one instance notes that a butterfly rash suggested a diagnosis of SLE, though it was ultimately attributed to another cause.

Clr4 is a histone methyltransferase that targets H3K9 and is required for heterochromatin assembly in fission yeast. It plays a role in encapsulating loci within transcriptionally inactive heterochromatin, regulating silencing at mating-type loci and affecting chromatin structure at centromeres. Sir2 deacetylates H3K14, which targets Clr4 to centromeres, and is critical for Clr4 function during de novo centromeric heterochromatin assembly.

LIMT is a novel long noncoding RNA that inhibits metastasis and is suppressed by EGF in breast cancer. Its low expression predicts poor patient prognosis, and it inhibits extracellular matrix invasion of mammary cells. The expression of LIMT is dependent on LiRos3 expression, and a functional LiMT/LiRos3 complex is pivotal in resistance towards MIL. Restoring LIMT expression can re-establish susceptibility to MIL.

No. While the text details various clinical manifestations of Fanconi anemia, including congenital malformations, bone marrow failure, and predisposition to malignancies, infertility is not mentioned as a characteristic feature of the disease. The provided information focuses on genome instability, hypersensitivity to DNA crosslinking, and associated cancer risks, but does not address reproductive health.

Kupffer cells are the resident macrophages of the liver and are involved in the body’s defense mechanisms. They release mediators and play a role in innate immunity, impacting hepatic metabolism and liver homeostasis. These cells can be selectively depleted to study their functional aspects in vivo, and their activity influences liver function and hepatocellular activity through signaling molecules like cAMP, PKC, and NF-kappaB.

Saponins are readily soluble in water and have been approved by the US FDA for inclusion in beverages intended for human consumption. *Jatropha curcas* seeds sold in Mexico for human consumption contain between 1.9-2.3% saponins, measured as diosgenin equivalent. Roasting seeds does not significantly reduce saponin levels, and they remain present even after processing. These compounds may offer a new intervention into the cycle of rotavirus infection by coating the epithelium of the small intestine and preventing viral attachment.

Yes, golimumab is effective for ulcerative colitis. It has demonstrated efficacy in inducing and maintaining clinical remission, clinical response, and mucosal healing in patients with the condition, and is approved for its treatment by the FDA. It is also effective in achieving clinical response and allowing for corticosteroid withdrawal in a significant percentage of patients.

H2A.W specifically marks heterochromatin and works with other marks to maintain transposon silencing. It enhances chromatin condensation by promoting interactions between chromatin fibers. Furthermore, H2A.W is required for heterochromatin condensation and appears to share common mechanisms for this process with animals, suggesting a conserved role in genome organization.

Hyperbilirubinemia can affect neonatal behavior and cause abnormal auditory reactions or abnormal muscle tonus. These effects may last for five months or more, and mild brain dysfunctions can be detected using the NBAS scale. Additionally, hyperbilirubinemia can lead to bilirubin-induced neurological dysfunctions and potentially alter neuroprotective barrier functions.

Yes, mTOR regulates MAPKAPK2 translation through 4EBP1, as revealed by a study uncovering a mechanism by which mTOR controls the senescence-associated secretory phenotype. This regulation impacts the phosphorylation of ZFP36L1 and subsequently affects SASP component transcripts.

Turner’s syndrome is most frequently caused by monosomy for the X chromosome, and can also result from deletion of the short arm of one X chromosome or the presence of a small supernumerary marker chromosome not derived from X or Y chromosomes.

The aim of the TRAP assay is to detect telomerase enzyme activity by extending an oligonucleotide through telomerase-mediated enzymatic addition of telomeric DNA repeats and subsequent PCR amplification of the extension products.

The PIM2 scoring system evaluates outcomes of patients admitted to pediatric intensive care units and can be used to assess risk-adjusted mortality. It functions as a beneficial tool for evaluating mortality risk and has adequate discriminatory function for case mixes of patients. Additionally, the system can be applied to predict mortality, though its contribution may be meager in some cases and recalibration may be needed, and it can be used in conjunction with other assessments like the Glasgow Coma Scale to understand long-term outcomes after injury.

No, mH2A levels are up-regulated during differentiation. It is stated that macro histone variants are expressed at low levels in stem cells and are up-regulated during differentiation, and that knockdown of these variants impaired differentiation.

Mutations in the SLC40A1 gene are associated with ferroportin disease, an inherited disorder of iron metabolism. This condition can manifest as haemochromatosis type 4, or HFE4, and presents with iron overload. Different SLC40A1 mutations appear to cause phenotypic variability within ferroportin disease, ranging from classical hyperferritinemia to more complex forms with hepatocellular iron deposits.

Yes, a glycine-to-arginine mutation in divalent metal transporter 1 results in microcytic, hypochromic anemia and systemic iron deficiency in Belgrade rats and severely impairs iron transport, leading to systemic iron deficiency and anemia in mice. Dysfunction of this transporter is also associated with iron deficiency anemia in humans.

No, the hERG gene codes for the cardiac I(Kr) potassium channel and is partly responsible for the return of the electric cardiac activity to the resting phase. It does not code for a sodium channel; the SCN5A gene codes for a cardiac sodium channel.

Yes, lenvatinib is effective for renal cell carcinoma. It improves progression-free survival in patients with metastatic renal cell carcinoma who have progressed after previous VEGF-targeted therapy, both when used alone and in combination with everolimus. It has also been approved by the FDA as a second-line treatment for advanced or metastatic renal cell carcinoma following prior VEGF-targeted therapy.
The NRD complex in S. cerevisiae consists of Nrd1, Nab3, and Sen1, and a novel complex was also identified as containing CHD3 and CHD4 proteins alongside histone deacetylase activity.
traseR is an R Bioconductor package used for performing trait-associated SNP enrichment analysis in genomic intervals.

Yes, HuR binds at 3' untranslated regions (UTRs) of target transcripts, protecting them against degradation, and also binds to adenine- and uridine-rich elements within the 3' untranslated regions of mRNAs. It specifically binds to one of five protein-binding segments in the 3'-untranslated region of the neurofibromin messenger RNA.

Exploding head syndrome is characterized by the perception of loud noises, often described as an explosion or bang, when falling asleep or waking up. These events are typically painless but can cause fear and distress, and may disrupt quality of life. While the cause is unknown, it is considered benign and can occur in both younger individuals, like college students, and those with other sleep disorders such as isolated sleep paralysis. Treatment options include reassurance, clomipramine, clobazam, or topiramate, though many sufferers require only reassurance.

Yes, H4K20 methylation is associated with DNA replication. It plays a direct role in recruiting proteins needed for the proper selection of DNA replication origins and is important for origin selection and activation, as well as ensuring the integrity of late replicating sequence domains. Disruption of the enzymes responsible for H4K20 methylation leads to defects in S phase progression and accumulation of DNA damage.

Crowned dens syndrome is a rare condition characterized by severe cervical pain and stiffness, often accompanied by fever and inflammatory markers. It involves crystalline deposition in the tissues around the odontoid process of the cervical spine, and can sometimes be misdiagnosed as other conditions like meningitis or rheumatoid arthritis. Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate deposition or hydroxy-apatite rheumatism can lead to this syndrome, and a cervical CT scan can aid in accurate diagnosis; symptoms typically subside within weeks with symptomatic therapy or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. In some instances, it can present with clivus destruction or erosion alongside a soft tissue mass with calcification.

Yes, CRISPR inversion of CTCF sites alters genome topology. Specifically, inverting CBS elements reconfigures the topology of chromatin loops between enhancers and promoters, and disrupts or destabilizes chromatin loops with distal, convergent CTCF sites.

Looped genes in yeast show high DNA bendability around the middle and 3/4 regions of their open reading frames. This bendability pattern is consistent between yeast species, though the exact location of the bendability peak can vary. Additionally, looped genes exhibit unstable nucleosome positioning around these middle regions of the open reading frame, which appears to be involved in loop formation. These characteristics assist in identifying looped genes.

The causes of the Koebner phenomenon are varied, with some researchers initially implicating infections or parasitic factors, though it is most commonly associated with trauma to the skin and is observed in numerous diseases including psoriasis, lichen planus, and sarcoidosis.

Yes, proton beam therapy is potentially important in the treatment of medulloblastoma. Several studies evaluate its use to reduce toxicity and improve outcomes compared to conventional X-ray radiotherapy, particularly concerning dose reduction to critical structures like the cochlea and spinal cord, and potentially reducing the risk of tumor recurrence or secondary cancers. It is estimated to be beneficial for approximately 20 children with medulloblastoma annually in Sweden.

Diacylglycerol O-acyltransferase 1 (DGAT1) catalyzes the final step of triglyceride synthesis in mammalian cells. It is considered a key enzyme controlling the rate of triglyceride synthesis and is the rate-limiting enzyme of this process. Variation in the DGAT1 gene may influence its expression and function, and it plays an important role in energy homeostasis and milk traits.
Histone H3-K27M mutation is associated with high-grade gliomas, particularly in children and young adults, and is a key driver of subtypes of gliomas with distinct clinical features.

No, the transcription of genes temporally regulated during development occurs in the absence of canonically active histone modifications. Furthermore, gain of structural loops and CTCF binding accompanies exit from pluripotency and delineates the spatial segregation of developmentally regulated genes, suggesting a shift away from reliance on active chromatin marks during development.

HaploReg v4 was developed to aid in the functional dissection of genome-wide association study results. It helps predict putative causal variants, likely cell types of action, and candidate target genes through systematic mining of various annotations. The tool incorporates expanded data like chromatin state maps, regulator binding data, and expression quantitative trait locus variants to understand the regulatory genome and translate genetic results into mechanistic insights.

Ménière’s disease is characterized by vestibular symptoms, auditory symptoms, pressure, hypoacusia, tinnitus, and dizziness, and can also be accompanied by panic attacks, agoraphobia, and anxiety, manifesting in a recurrent and unforeseeable course.
Ehlers-Danlos syndromes primarily affect soft connective tissue, including components of the extracellular matrix and fibrillar collagens.

Mondor's disease is a rare, benign condition characterized by superficial thrombophlebitis—inflammation of a vein—that can occur in the thoracoabdominal and genital areas, most frequently affecting the thoracic wall and female breast. While its cause is often unknown, it can be related to trauma, operation, or biopsy procedures. The disease typically resolves on its own or with local treatment, and it is not associated with cancer or systemic disease.

Metalloenzyme inhibitors include hydroxamic acids, carboxylates, hydroxypyridinonates, thiols, N-hydroxyureas, foscarnet, BB-3497, VT-1129, and VT-1161, alongside compounds like 2,2'-dipyridylamine and triazacyclononane as well as nikkomycin Z and E-1210.

Immediate-early genes are the first viral genes expressed after infection or reactivation, and their transcription does not require prior viral protein synthesis. They are experimentally defined by their transcription following infection or reactivation when protein synthesis is inhibited.  Furthermore, IEGs are rapidly induced, such as in response to growth factor stimulations, and this induction can lead to co-upregulation of neighboring genes. Their expression can be visualized through techniques like fluorescence in situ hybridization to map functional brain imaging with cellular resolution.
The INO80 remodeling complex and the histone chaperone ANP32E remove H2A.Z from chromatin in yeast, and the SWR1 complex replaces conventional histone H2A with histone H2A.Z.
libFLASM is a free open-source C++ software library used for solving fixed-length approximate string matching under both the edit and Hamming distance models.

Yes, a method exists for generating highly multiplexed ChIP-seq libraries that involves converting Y-shaped sequencing adapters to double stranded DNA before agarose gel size selection and quantifying amplification cycles with qPCR to avoid over-amplification. Barcoding libraries is also an essential part of experimental design, allowing for the sequencing of multiple samples per lane and reducing technical bias.

Yes, doxorubicin is cardiotoxic. It is described as an effective anticancer agent with known cardiotoxic side effects and frequently causes dose-related cardiotoxic effects, including cardiomyopathy and heart failure. Research focuses on reducing these cardiotoxic effects while maintaining its antitumor properties.

Primary intestinal lymphangiectasia is associated with lymphoma, Hennekam syndrome, and potentially conditions related to genes and regulatory molecules for lymphangiogenesis, as well as a possible predisposition to chronic viral infections like those induced by human papilloma virus resulting in generalized warts.

No, the mouse Sry gene locus contains repetitive sequences, including (CA)n and (CAG) repeats, and is located within an inverted repeat structure. It also lies near a large inverted repeat and contains unique sequences within that repeat.

Yes, Meis1 coordinates a network of genes implicated in eye development and microphthalmia, and haploinsufficiency of Meis1 causes microphthalmic traits and visual impairment in mice. It is also responsible for human microphthalmia and is at the core of a genetic network implicated in eye patterning/microphthalmia.

Yes, persistent elevation of Pentraxin 3 levels following initial infection appears to predict poor patient outcome and may contribute to disease sequelae, and measurement of Pentraxin 3 following sepsis onset may improve patient risk assessment. Additionally, plasma Pentraxin 3 levels are elevated in sepsis and correlate with mortality. It also predicts severity of infections in acute decompensated cirrhotic patients.
RNA polymerase II transcribes pri-miRNAs, although RNA polymerase III can also transcribe them in some instances, such as in murine gamma-herpesvirus 68.
Mutations in the tyrosinase gene, the key enzyme in melanin synthesis, are responsible for oculocutaneous albinism type 1.
Treatments for a torn rotator cuff include arthroscopic subacromial decompression and debridement, as well as open repair with acromioplasty.

Laser interstitial thermal therapy is a minimally invasive treatment option for gliomas, including high-grade gliomas that are difficult to access. It utilizes controlled thermal damage to tumor cells and can be used as an alternative to traditional surgery, especially for patients who are high risk for more invasive procedures. While complete resection is often unattainable, LITT may have a similar impact on outcomes, and is currently being assessed in randomized trials to determine its efficacy and long-term benefits for progression-free survival. It has shown feasibility for a variety of brain pathologies and can be delivered upfront or as a salvage therapy.
The SWR1 complex, with Swr1 as its catalytic core, mediates the replacement of histone H2A with histone H2A.Z in the yeast *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*.
Mcidas and GemC1/Lynkeas are key players in radial glial specification to ependymal cells, operating upstream of c-Myb and FoxJ1, and are sufficient and necessary for ependymal cell generation.

Yes, ocular melanosis is a risk factor for uveal melanoma. It is often underestimated as a risk factor, and uveal melanoma was detected in all four cases of ocular melanosis presented. Additionally, individuals with ocular/oculodermal melanocytosis have a higher risk of developing uveal melanoma, with an estimated rate of 1 in 400 affected patients. It can also degenerate into melanosarcomas, with 10% of cases doing so.

Higher levels of perceived stress are associated with shorter telomere length, and accumulating major life stressors predicts telomere attrition. However, engaging in healthy behaviors may mitigate the effects of stress on telomere shortening, appearing to offer protection. Specifically, for every major life stressor experienced, there was a greater decline in telomere length, though this effect was lessened in women with higher levels of health behaviors. Cross-sectionally, stressed postmenopausal women have shorter telomeres, but this is only observed in those who are inactive.
The ackee fruit causes Jamaican vomiting sickness through the ingestion of its unripe arils, seeds, and husks, and contains a toxic principle called hypoglycin A.
Mutations in the PLA2G6 gene are causative of Karak syndrome and other PLA2G6-associated neurodegeneration.

LedPred is a flexible support vector machine workflow designed to predict new regulatory sequences. It is based on the annotation of known cis-regulatory modules and integrates heterogeneous data like position-specific scoring matrices, ChIP-seq data, and conservation scores. This package is available as an R/Bioconductor package and an online server, and it performs well in predicting regulatory sequences in Drosophila and mouse datasets.

Yes, siltuximab appears to be a safe and effective treatment for Castleman disease, particularly for those with newly diagnosed or previously treated conditions. Studies demonstrate it significantly reduces disease burden and symptoms, with a higher rate of durable tumor and symptomatic response compared to placebo, leading to approvals from the FDA and EMA for its use in treating the disease. It has shown efficacy in patients with multicentric Castleman disease and can lead to symptom disappearance for an extended period.

Degrons are elements used by E3 ubiquitin ligases to target proteins for degradation. They are often short linear motifs embedded within proteins and function as regulatory sites for protein abundance, impacting cellular processes. These signals regulate protein turnover via the ubiquitin-proteasome system and can be comprised of a primary motif, neighboring ubiquitinated lysines, and a structurally disordered segment. Some degrons can even function across species, initiating protein unfolding and subsequent degradation by the proteasome.
SOX2 anophthalmia syndrome, characterized by severe eye malformations and extraocular anomalies involving the brain, esophagus, and genitalia, is associated with mutations in the SOX2 gene.

Yes, EPRS is phosphorylated at multiple sites, including Ser(886) and Ser(999), and phosphorylation is essential for its function in the GAIT complex and its release from the tRNA multisynthetase complex. The context details specific kinases involved in these phosphorylation events, such as Cdk5 and a Cdk5-dependent-AGC kinase.

Necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum is associated with diabetes mellitus, and more specifically, it is frequently found alongside other chronic complications of diabetes and can be linked to poor glucose control.

Lysine 2-hydroxyisobutyrylation (Khib) is a histone mark found at 63 sites in humans and mice, with 27 being unique lysine sites not modified by acetylation or crotonylation. This mark appears to be associated with active gene transcription, particularly in meiotic and post-meiotic cells, and induces structural changes to chromatin. Findings suggest Khib plays a critical role in regulating chromatin functions due to its conservation, widespread distribution, and high stoichiometry.

Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and neuropsychiatric diseases are associated with the Dopamine Receptor D4, and polymorphisms in the DRD4 gene present a genetic risk factor for ADHD, particularly in Egyptian and Asian populations.

Corynebacterium diphtheriae and Corynebacterium ulcerans bacteria cause diphtheria, with the diphtheria toxin produced by these bacteria potentially damaging cranial nerves and inducing macrophage apoptosis as mechanisms of pathogenic effect.

The calcium-sensing receptor plays a pivotal role in systemic calcium metabolism by regulating parathyroid hormone secretion and urinary calcium excretion, and it also regulates diverse processes such as hormone secretion, gene expression, inflammation, proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis.
The CHAMP trial evaluated amitriptyline and topiramate, along with a placebo, for the prevention of migraine in children and adolescents.

IL-18 binding protein is a naturally occurring inhibitor of IL-18 that acts as a negative feedback mechanism, controlling the biological activities of IL-18 and potentially ameliorating inflammatory responses and tissue damage.
TFIIB and intrinsic DNA bendability assist in the formation of gene loops in the yeast *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*.

The ARISTOTLE trial compared apixaban to warfarin in patients with atrial fibrillation, demonstrating apixaban’s superiority in reducing stroke or systemic embolism, all-cause mortality, and major bleeding.

REVIGO is a web server that summarizes and visualizes long lists of gene ontology terms. It finds a representative subset of these terms using a clustering algorithm based on semantic similarity measures. REVIGO also visualizes this non-redundant set of terms through multidimensional scaling, graph-based visualizations, treemaps, and tag clouds to aid in interpretation. It is freely available online at http://revigo.irb.hr/.

Onartuzumab is a monovalent antibody that inhibits hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) binding to the MET receptor tyrosine kinase, thereby blocking receptor phosphorylation and signaling. Specifically, it prevents the binding of the HGF α-chain to MET, which is crucial for MET signaling and dimerization. By blocking HGF-induced MET activation, onartuzumab demonstrates antitumor activity and can improve the efficacy of other treatments like erlotinib, particularly in cases of erlotinib resistance caused by elevated HGF expression.

Yes, B cells are implicated in the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis. They are found in the CSF of patients with the disease, and anti-B-cell therapies have shown efficacy in treatment. Furthermore, EBV, which modulates B cell function, is strongly linked to an increased risk of developing multiple sclerosis, and B cell responses correlate with disease activity. Short-lived plasma blasts, a B cell subset, are present at high numbers in the CSF of multiple sclerosis patients and correlate with disease activity and inflammation.
Molluscum contagiosum is caused by the molluscum contagiosum virus, which is a member of the poxvirus family.

Mutations in the HEX A gene cause Tay-Sachs disease, as well as juvenile, chronic, and adult GM2 gangliosidoses. Hexosaminidase A deficiency can also result in progressive cerebellar ataxia, proximal neurogenic weakness, and ocular motor disturbances. Some deficiencies are less severe variants with some residual Hex A activity, and these are most prevalent among Ashkenazi Jews.

The provided text does not contain information about Behçet's disease. It discusses Paget's disease, MRSA, Crohn's disease, Tau protein, and a patient approach involving dermatologists, psychiatrists, and counselors, but does not mention Behçet's disease or its characteristics. Therefore, a description of the disease cannot be formulated from this information.

Yes, Yersinia pestis causes a fatal respiratory disease called pneumonic plague and can also cause secondary pneumonia following infection by flea bite. It is capable of infecting the lungs at a very early stage in its evolution and can cause primary pulmonic plague through aerosol transmission.

Bupropion is clinically used as a treatment for depression, tobacco use cessation, and is being tested as a candidate treatment for psychostimulant drug abuse, attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder, and obesity.

BAX protein plays a key role in mitochondria-mediated apoptosis and is a major proapoptotic member of the Bcl2 family required for apoptotic cell death, functioning through conformational changes, insertion into mitochondrial membranes, and oligomerization leading to cytochrome c release.

Ctf4 recruits the Chl1 helicase to the replisome through a conserved interaction motif. While Chl1 facilitates replication fork progression, its role in sister chromatid cohesion requires Ctf4 interaction, not helicase activity. Both Ctf4 and Chl1 contribute to cohesin acetylation, and their absence causes similar growth defects in cells lacking Eco1, suggesting they function in a shared pathway for cohesion establishment.

The cause of primary intestinal lymphangiectasia (PIL) remains unknown, though some research suggests links to genes and regulatory molecules involved in lymphangiogenesis. It is characterized by dilated intestinal lymphatics, leading to lymph leakage into the small bowel. While very rare familial cases have been reported, and a deletion on chromosome 4 has been observed in one patient, the etiology is generally not understood.

Congenital disorders of glycosylation are a growing group of inherited metabolic diseases affecting glycoconjugates, and can result from defects in protein N-glycosylation pathways. These disorders are often multisystemic, impacting proteins throughout the organism due to mutations in a single gene. They can manifest as weakness present at birth, neuronal migration defects, or a range of other issues like facial dysmorphism, cardiac defects, and neurological involvement, and frequently impact the central and/or peripheral nervous systems.

The JASPAR 2016 release expanded the CORE collection with 494 new transcription factor binding profiles—315 in vertebrates, 11 in nematodes, 3 in insects, 1 in fungi, and 164 in plants—and updated 59 profiles, introduced 130 transcription factor flexible models trained on ChIP-seq data for vertebrates, provided a new web tool for inferring binding profiles, and offered a Ruby module complementing the JASPAR API for easier programmatic access.

Tafazzin is a phospholipid transacylase involved in cardiolipin metabolism and remodeling, specifically by shuffling fatty acids between molecular species. This activity supports changes in mitochondrial membrane conformation and is critical for the differentiation of cardiomyocytes, contributing to the characteristic cristae-rich morphology of cardiac mitochondria. Mutations in tafazzin cause Barth syndrome, and the protein participates in acyl-transfer reactions between cardiolipin and phosphatidylcholine without requiring CoA or acyl-CoA as substrates.

Assassin bugs are members of the family Reduviidae, which is a diverse group of true bugs containing over 6,000 species.  The Triatominae subfamily of assassin bugs, also known as kissing bugs, feed on vertebrate blood and are potential vectors of Chagas disease. Their mitochondrial genomes typically consist of 37 genes and a control region, exhibiting conserved genomic organization across different species.

No, the genes for marneral biosynthesis are found in an operon-like cluster in *Arabidopsis thaliana*. The context describes characterizing a second operon-like triterpene cluster, the marneral cluster, and comparing it to another cluster, suggesting these genes are grouped together rather than scattered.

Yes, dupilumab is FDA approved for treatment of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis and has shown marked improvement in patient symptoms, including pruritus and clinically visible disease, in clinical trials. It is the first biologic approved for this purpose and is considered a truly innovative therapeutic approach for the condition.
Base J is found in telomeric repeats, regions where transcription starts and stops, and is picked up in DNA segments inserted into plasmids grown in *Leishmania tarentolae*.
LedPred is an R/bioconductor package that predicts regulatory sequences using support vector machines and integrates heterogeneous data such as ChIP-seq data.

Unipolar brush cells are a class of interneurons found in the granular layer of the mammalian cerebellum. They are small neurons, intermediate in size between granule and Golgi cells, and are remarkably similar across all lobules. These cells are enriched in the vestibulo-cerebellum, but are also found at low density in the lateral regions of the cerebellar hemispheres and the dorsal paraflocculus, as well as in the cochlear nucleus. They reside nearly exclusively in the granular layer.

Driver mutations identified for Diffuse Intrinsic Pontine Glioma include histone 3 (H3) K27M—specifically H3.2K27M—alterations in the TP53 cell-cycle pathway (TP53/PPM1D), alterations in specific growth factor pathways (ACVR1/PIK3R1), recurrent activating ACVR1 mutations, and c.83A>T mutations in the H3F3A or HIST1H3B gene.

Yes, Jarid2 is required for pre-implantation development and is important for embryonic stem cell differentiation, which is essential for early embryo development. It coordinates Nanog expression and PCP/Wnt signaling, both of which are required for efficient ESC differentiation and early embryo development. Additionally, lacking Jarid2 induces multiple ICM formation when injected into blastocysts.

Yes, PUVA therapy is indicated for eczema treatment. Both oral and bath PUVA are effective for chronic palmoplantar eczema, and localized PUVA is used for vesicular dyshidrotic eczema. Additionally, topical PUVA therapy can induce partial remission in patients with hyperkeratotic eczema who do not respond to conventional treatments. Dermatologists frequently prescribe PUVA, particularly in Flanders, for eczema.

DECKO is a dual CRISPR tool used for deleting genomic elements, including long non-coding RNAs. It utilizes a vector system and two guide RNAs (gRNAs) and is cloned using a single oligonucleotide, offering simplicity and scalability for CRISPR knockout studies. This tool successfully deleted genomic fragments ranging from 100 to 3000 bp and resulted in reductions of up to 98% in MALAT1 RNA levels, alongside reduced proliferation rates.

ANGPTL3 and APOB are genes associated with hypolipidemia, with ANGPTL3 mutations responsible for a combined hypolipidemia phenotype and loss-of-function mutations causing recessive hypolipidemia, while mutations of the APOB gene are absent in studied cohorts.

PARL functions as a mitochondrial protease with catalytic activity involving both a triad-based mechanism utilizing a conserved aspartic acid residue and γ-cleavage, a proteolytic event that can disrupt its own proteolytic activity.

Yes, brown adipose cells produce heat by means of uncoupler protein 1 (UCP1) from fatty acids and sugar, and both classical brown fat and beige cells are activated to catalyze stored lipids and carbohydrates to produce heat via UCP1 in response to cold. Oxidation of fat also proceeds in brown fat tissue for thermogenesis.

A mimotope vaccine utilizes peptide mimics of epitopes recognized by antibodies with antitumor activity as potential candidates for cancer immunotherapy. These vaccines are designed to induce a polyclonal antibody response focused on the target of the original antibody. They can be generated by coupling a mimotope to an immunogenic carrier, and have shown potential in inhibiting tumor growth both in vitro and in vivo, sometimes in combination with other immune-stimulating agents like IL-15 and IL-21.

Mal de Debarquement Syndrome is a neurological disorder of motion perception triggered by external motion, and is characterized by a persistent sensation of rocking and swaying, often felt upon return to stable land after travel. Symptoms can last for months to years and include mild unsteadiness and anxiety, though it is typically a short-lived phenomenon in most individuals. While often mild, the syndrome can also manifest as a subjective perception of self-motion after exposure to passive motion, and is associated with high levels of depression and illness intrusiveness.

Yes, melioidosis is an infectious disease caused by the Gram-negative bacterium Burkholderia pseudomallei, and it is a suppurative chronic infection caused by this bacterium. The disease is also caused by the saprophytic bacterium Burkholderia pseudomallei.

Verubecestat is a BACE-1 inhibitor that reduces the levels of Aβ peptides, including Aβ40 and Aβ42, found in the cerebrospinal fluid and brain. It works by inhibiting the β-site amyloid precursor protein cleaving enzyme 1 (BACE1), which is required for the production of Aβ. By suppressing the accumulation of these Aβ peptides and plaque load, verubecestat aims to modify the disease progression of Alzheimer’s disease.

Triple negative gliomas represent a distinct molecular group within gliomas. They occur in both grade II/III and grade IV gliomas, comprising 7% of grade II/III tumors and 17% of grade IV tumors. These gliomas are characterized by the absence of mutations in IDH, TERT, and 1p/19q codeletion, and are associated with a worse prognosis in grade II gliomas. In adults, triple negative gliomas are also linked to IDH-independent gliomagenesis.

Ectopia lentis is a genetically caused condition involving the subluxation of the lens, resulting from disruption of the zonular fibers. It often leads to a marked loss in visual acuity and can be accompanied by ocular complications like cataract, myopia, and retinal detachment. This condition can occur as an isolated symptom or as part of syndromal disorders such as Marfan syndrome or homocystinuria. It is usually autosomal dominant, but can also be autosomal recessive depending on the genetic cause.

Yes, glyburide may decrease cerebral edema by blocking SUR1 receptors and appears to reduce vasogenic edema in stroke patients and those with brain metastases, potentially offering an alternative to dexamethasone. Studies indicate it diminishes lesional water diffusivity and reduces blood MMP-9 levels, while also decreasing BTB permeability.

Gasdermin D is an executor of pyroptosis, a lytic form of programmed cell death, and is crucial for interleukin-1β secretion. It is cleaved by inflammatory caspases, and this cleavage is required for pyroptosis to occur. The N-terminal fragment of gasdermin D can trigger cell death and possesses membrane pore-forming activity, ultimately leading to cell lysis. Gasdermin D is also a substrate of both caspase-1 and caspase-11/4/5.

TOPAZ1 is a novel gene specifically expressed in germ cells and is conserved across vertebrates. It encodes a protein containing PAZ and CCCH domains, and is highly expressed in the gonads during germ cell meiosis. Inactivation of Topaz1 in mice results in male sterility, indicating it is essential for male meiotic progression, and it influences the expression of approximately one hundred transcripts. It may also play a role in the piRNA pathway and contribute to genome integrity.

Symptoms of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency include haemolytic anaemia, persistent unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, and, in some cases, impaired fasting glucose and diabetes, though some individuals may be asymptomatic and avoid haemolytic diatheses with avoidance of oxidant stress.

Yes, the gorilla genome sequence has been determined and analyzed, with a high-quality assembly generated using single-molecule sequencing technology, and comparisons made to other great ape genomes. Further analysis has also focused on specific genes and DNA sequences within the gorilla genome.

Yes, vemurafenib is used for thyroid cancer. Several documents discuss its use in treating BRAF-mutant thyroid cancer, though resistance to the drug can occur. Clinical studies are underway to evaluate its effectiveness, and it has shown promise in trials despite cellular resistance. It is also being tested in papillary and anaplastic thyroid cancers.

No. The provided information details associations between C9ORF72 mutations and frontotemporal lobar degeneration, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, psychosis, and behavioral characteristics, but there is no mention of any connection to macular degeneration.

The Genome 10K Project aims to sequence the genomes of 10,000 vertebrates, representing approximately one genome for each vertebrate genus. It was established in 2009 by a consortium of biologists and genome scientists and has already sequenced or initiated genomes for 277 species as of mid-2014. A significant focus of the project is to sequence 4,000 fish genomes, as fishes represent over 50% of extant vertebrates, with nearly 60 already being sequenced and another 100 identified for future sequencing. The project intends to deliver genome empowerment for many vertebrate species to future generations.
HDAC1 modulates GATA-1 transcriptional activity through direct binding and deacetylation, impacting its chromatin binding and activity.

MIRA-seq is a reliable, genome-scale DNA methylation analysis platform used for scoring DNA methylation differences at CpG-rich genomic regions. It combines the methylated-CpG island recovery assay (MIRA) with next generation sequencing platforms. The method utilizes the high affinity of the MBD2b-MBD3L1 complex for CpG-methylated DNA and is cost effective, not limited by primer or probe design. It can also be used to analyze genome-wide DNA methylation patterns between different groups, such as Holstein and Angus cattle.

Ssu72 is required for gene looping and physically associates with both promoter and terminator regions of genes. It interacts with Pta1, a component of the CPF 3'-end processing complex, and this interaction is necessary for looping to occur. Specifically, Ssu72-mediated CTD dephosphorylation facilitates transcription reinitiation within these looped structures, and its depletion adversely affects looping alongside CPF components like Pta1.

Yes, the context describes mutations related to several conditions – red hair (up to five loss-of-function mutations in MC1R), Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy (mutations in STA and LMNA genes), and smallpox prophylaxis (no specific mutation number is given, but outbreaks are controlled with vaccination coverage above 70%). These examples do not suggest a number of described human nuclear mutations exceeding 50000.

Peptide aptamers are combinatorial protein molecules that specifically bind to target proteins, even under intracellular conditions. They can interfere with protein functions and modulate both proteins and cell functions, varying from in vitro detection to in vivo modulation. Furthermore, peptide aptamers can inhibit gene products without mutating DNA and have demonstrated anticancer and antivirus activity, showing promise as therapeutic molecules.

Clinical symptoms of left ventricular noncompaction include signs of left ventricular systolic dysfunction potentially leading to heart failure, ventricular arrhythmias, embolic events, thromboembolic complications, and, rarely, acute cerebral ischemia.

The Glasgow Coma Scale is used to classify the level of coma and appears to be a gold standard for this purpose. It is utilized in neurologic rehabilitation to assess a person’s clarity of consciousness and provides information for structuring the rehabilitation process. This scale is also a component of other scoring systems, like the Revised Trauma Scoring system and the GAP scoring system, and is associated with predicting both mortality and functional outcome in patients with brain injury or hemorrhage.
Andexanet alfa is an antidote targeted to reverse the oral direct factor Xa inhibitors as well as the indirect inhibitor enoxaparin.
The main cause of Patau syndrome is trisomy 13, often resulting from non-separation during meiosis in older mothers, though it can also occur as a mosaic or translocation.

Duplication of the SOX9 gene can cause XX sex reversal, leading to the development of male characteristics in individuals with an XX karyotype and, in some cases, abnormalities such as penile/scrotal hypospadias or testicular tissue development.

Yes, musclin is described as a novel skeletal muscle-derived secretory factor and a muscle-derived secretory peptide, responsive to insulin, found in the signal sequence trap of mouse skeletal muscle cDNAs.
Connective tissue is commonly affected in Marfan's syndrome, often due to mutations in the fibrillin-1 gene that disrupt elastic fibers in arteries and other tissues.
Loss of CD28 expression by liver-infiltrating T cells contributes to the pathogenesis of primary sclerosing cholangitis.

No, familial porphyria cutanea tarda is described as a low-penetrance autosomal dominant condition, and erythropoietic protoporphyria can be linked to co-inheritance of a functional polymorphism with a null allele, suggesting a dominant component to its inheritance. The context does not indicate that cutaneous porphyrias are inherited with a recessive pattern.
ZMapp is a treatment for Ebola virus disease, formerly known as a hemorrhagic fever.

Yes, severe deficiency of methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR) is the most common inborn error of folate metabolism and is biochemically characterized by homocystinuria. Several mutations in the MTHFR gene can lead to this severe enzymatic deficiency.

H2AX is phosphorylated on a serine residue, forming γ-H2AX, as an early event in the chromatin response to DNA double-strand breaks and extends over large regions of DNA from the break site, visualized as foci with specific antibodies.

Yes, several studies and meta-analyses suggest valproic acid can prolong survival in glioblastoma patients. Evidence indicates a hazard ratio of 0.56, confirming a benefit of VPA use, and sub-group analysis shows a hazard ratio of 0.74 compared to other AEDs. Additionally, retrospective studies have reported improved survival with its use, potentially due to histone deacetylase inhibition, and it has been shown to improve overall and progression-free survival in patients receiving chemoradiation therapy.

The direct interaction of Ikaros and Foxp1 modulates the expression of the G protein-coupled receptor G2A, enhances Gpr132 transcription, and causes cell cycle changes including G2 arrest in B-lymphocytes.
Mice lacking Pds5B exhibit developmental abnormalities reminiscent of Cornelia de Lange syndrome.
Barth syndrome is an X-linked recessive disorder that affects only boys, and is caused by mutations in the G4.5 gene located on the X chromosome.

Yes, skin lesions frequently occur in HIV-positive patients, and Cryptococcus neoformans var. neoformans serotype A predominates as the etiological agent in these cases. Additionally, Cryptococcus should be considered in the differential diagnosis of subcutaneous necrosis in patients on immunosuppressive therapy.

Pictilisib is an oral class I phosphatidylinositol-3-phosphate kinase inhibitor. It functions by reducing phosphorylation of AKT in platelet rich plasma. While addition of pictilisib to fulvestrant did not improve progression-free survival, its dosing was limited by toxicity, suggesting a potent but challenging mechanism of action.

Sirtuins are a class of proteins initially discovered as being similar to a yeast protein and retained in mammals, with seven identified in humans. They function as NAD+-dependent histone deacetylases and are involved in nutrient sensing and metabolic function. These enzymes regulate numerous downstream enzymes and play critical roles in cellular regulation, impacting diseases like cancer, neurodegenerative disease, and metabolic disorders.
The provided text does not contain information about any miRNA targeted by SRY/Sox9.
Transcription factor activity analysis identified HOXD10 as being specifically activated after VEGFR-3 stimulation and regulating expression of immediate early transcription factors.

Yes, obeticholic acid is used for the treatment of primary biliary cholangitis. It has received accelerated approval in the USA, either in combination with ursodeoxycholic acid for those with an inadequate response, or as monotherapy for those unable to tolerate ursodeoxycholic acid. Clinical trials have also demonstrated that it significantly decreases serum alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin in patients with the disease, particularly when used with ursodeoxycholic acid or as a monotherapy for UDCA non-responders.
Vanoxerine has been repositioned for the treatment of atrial fibrillation and flutter.

The No Promoter Left Behind method is an organism-independent approach for characterizing diverse promoter architectures directly from experimentally identified genome-wide TSSs without relying on known promoter elements and has been applied to identifying novel architectures in the fly genome.

Mutations in the FBN1 and ADAMTSL4 genes are associated with isolated ectopia lentis, with FBN1 mutations typically causing an autosomal dominant form and ADAMTSL4 mutations resulting in autosomal recessive inheritance, though a homozygous deletion in ADAMTSL4 has also been linked to craniosynostosis with ectopia lentis.

No, ovine refers to sheep. One study details Jaagsiekte sheep retrovirus and ovine pulmonary adenocarcinoma, establishing a clear link between “ovine” and sheep. Additionally, a study specifically mentions the sheep (Ovis aries) H19 gene.

Yes, GATA-1 regulates ribosomal protein genes because a failure in the secretory pathway leads to repression of both rRNA and ribosomal protein genes, and protein kinase C (PKC) deletion relieves this repression. Additionally, single cell RNA-seq analysis reveals changes in signature gene expression patterns in hematopoietic stem cells, myeloid progenitors, and erythroid/megakaryocyte clusters, indicating GATA-1’s role in regulating gene expression related to these cell types which utilize ribosomal proteins.
Mutations in the fibrillin-1 (FBN1) gene, and heterozygosity for mutations in the TGFBR1 or TGFBR2 genes cause Marfan syndrome.
ARCALYST is approved for the treatment of two CAPS disorders, FCAS and MWS in adults and children 12 years and older.

ChIPpeakAnno is a Bioconductor package within the R statistical programming environment designed to facilitate the batch annotation of enriched peaks from experiments like ChIP-seq, ChIP-chip, and CAGE. It allows for visualizing annotated binding sites as tables, pie charts, or histograms, including the distribution of distances to nearby genes. The package also provides tools for assessing overlap significance between replicates or transcription factors and retrieving sequences for further analysis like PCR amplification or motif discovery.

Dupilumab inhibits the signaling of interleukin-4 (IL-4) and interleukin-13 (IL-13) pathways, as well as the IL-4/IL-13-dependent signal transduction and the IL-4 receptor alpha subunit (IL-4Rα) signaling.

Yes, a high TOP2B/TOP2A expression ratio at diagnosis correlates with favorable outcomes, specifically longer disease-free and overall survival, for patients undergoing standard chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukemia (AML). This ratio was significantly correlated with treatment outcomes in a multivariate analysis.

The three main structures of the cytoskeleton are actin filaments, microtubules, and intermediate filaments, which are fibrillar polymers constantly changing their organization during cellular activities.
The genus for the common European honey bee is *Apis*.
The TAZ (G4.5) gene is located on Xq28 in humans.

Nerve-associated peripheral glial progenitors give rise to parasympathetic neurons, including trunk ganglia and the cranial ciliary, pterygopalatine, lingual, submandibular, and otic ganglia, as well as glia and neurons.

The number of topological associated domains contained in the human Hox cluster is not explicitly stated, but the context indicates these domains are mostly constitutive and may have been used as genomic niches where novel regulations could evolve around Hox loci.

No, Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome typically begins in early to mid-childhood and has a peak period for manifestations between ages 3 and 5 years. While the condition can continue into adulthood, a study followed patients with a mean age of 43.3 years *at their last evaluation*, indicating they were diagnosed much earlier in life. The context also describes studies involving children aged between 3 and 9 years with the syndrome.

The EXCEL trial compared percutaneous coronary intervention with everolimus-eluting stents to coronary artery bypass graft surgery in patients with left main coronary artery disease and a SYNTAX score of 32 or less, to determine if PCI could provide comparable or superior outcomes to the standard of care, CABG.

ATR-16 syndrome is caused by alterations on chromosome 16p13.3 and is often accompanied by alpha-thalassemia, mild-to-moderate mental retardation, and dysmorphic facial features. It can also involve skeletal and genitourinary malformations, minor anomalies, and developmental delay. The syndrome is typically due to a deletion of the distal short arm of chromosome 16, though complex rearrangements involving duplications are also observed.
The provided text does not contain information about the results of inactivated ANGPLT3.
Between 70 and 75 percent of rheumatoid arthritis patients show a significant clinical response to anti-TNF therapies, though 20 to 30 percent do not respond sufficiently to a given anti-TNF drug.
Semaglutide is used for the treatment of type 2 diabetes and obesity.
SUDEP refers to the sudden unexpected death of a seemingly healthy individual with epilepsy, usually occurring during or immediately after a tonic-clonic seizure.
The yeast cleavage and polyadenylation complex contains the major poly(A) polymerase Pap1, as well as the alternative poly(A) polymerase Trf4, and also includes CPSF 160 and 73 subunits.
Vestibular schwannomas can be categorized using the Koos grading system.
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency has an X-linked inheritance.

BiRen, PEDLA, DEEP, EP-DNN, and DeepLoc are deep learning-based algorithms used for enhancer prediction, with BiRen predicting enhancers using DNA sequence alone, PEDLA learning from heterogeneous data across cell types, DEEP integrating diverse characteristics and features, and EP-DNN utilizing chromatin features in different cell types.

No, beta-thalassemia is associated with reduced or absent expression of the beta-globin gene, and some cases involve mutations or deletions within or around this gene, or upstream regulatory sequences affecting its expression. One report details an individual with beta-thalassemia who did not have any deletions at the alpha-globin locus, suggesting the condition isn’t directly linked to alterations in alpha-globin genes.

Yes, cardiolipin is described as the specific phospholipid of mitochondria and a universal component of mitochondrial membranes in all eukaryotes, playing an important role in their structural organization and function. Additionally, studies have observed increased percentages of cardiolipin in aged cardiac mitochondrial membranes.

No. Several studies demonstrate associations between DNA methylation patterns and Post Traumatic Stress Disorder, investigating genes like Bdnf, Dlgap2, SKA2, MAN2C1, LINE-1, and Alu, and finding alterations in methylation linked to PTSD vulnerability, resilience, or symptom development.

Yes, POLD3 is essential for mouse development, as it is required for viability in adult animals and Pold3(+/-) mice were born at sub-Mendelian ratios with some presenting hydrocephaly and a reduced lifespan.

Romosozumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that binds to sclerostin, a glycoprotein that inhibits bone formation. By binding to sclerostin, romosozumab prevents it from blocking the Wnt signaling pathway, thereby activating this pathway and increasing bone formation and bone mineral density. Additionally, romosozumab uniquely suppresses bone resorption, contributing to its effects on bone strength.

The Davidson Trauma Scale is used to assess post-traumatic stress disorder symptoms, and can also be used to evaluate depressive symptoms as some PTSD patients show values indicative of major depressive episode.

The Mis18 protein is a critical player in determining when and where centromeres are built and is essential for proper centromere function. It forms a complex with Mis16 and is required for the recruitment of CENP-A to centromeres, acting as a loading factor for this histone variant. This complex recruits HJURP (Scm3) to facilitate CENP-A assembly, and its localization to centromeres is regulated by interactions with other proteins like Mis18BP1 and CENP-C.

No, hepadnaviral minichromosomes are not free of nucleosomes. They contain a full complement of nucleosomes and can even assemble new ones during replication, ranging from half to fully chromatinized due to their association with variable numbers of nucleosomes. Nucleosome-protected sites have also been consistently identified within the viral genome in these minichromosomes.

No. The information describes Cri Du Chat syndrome as resulting from a deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5, or from recombination aneusomy involving chromosome 5, including monosomy and trisomy. There is no mention of repeat expansions within genes on chromosome 5 being associated with the syndrome. The descriptions focus on missing genetic material, not altered repeat sequences.

Smyd3 is required for the development of cardiac and skeletal muscle in zebrafish, plays an important role in heart and skeletal muscle development alongside SETD7 where both genes are highly expressed within the developing heart and proper activity is essential for normal heart morphogenesis, and its methyltransferase activity modifies histone H3 lysine 4.
Cyclin dependent kinase 4/6 is inhibited by ribociclib, and CDK4 and 6 are potent targets of the drug.

Histone H3 mutations, specifically K27M and G34R/V, as well as K36M and G34W/L mutations in genes encoding the replacement histone H3.3, and mutations affecting histone components H3F3A p.K27M or p.G34 have been associated with pediatric high-grade gliomas, with K27M-H3.3 being prevalent in diffuse intrinsic pontine gliomas and linked to shorter survival.

Yes, it is feasible to obtain DNA read lengths exceeding 30 kb. One document states third-generation sequencing platforms can generate exceptionally long reads, over 20 kb, and another reports a maximum read length of 80 kb was produced using a specific protocol.

No, osteocrin is not exclusively expressed in bone. While it is highly expressed in bone and associated with osteoblast lineage cells, it is also expressed in the primate brain and regulates neuronal structure and function in response to sensory input.

Achondroplasia is a congenital short-limbed dwarfing condition, the most common form of which has a birth prevalence of 1 in 20000-30000 live-born infants, characterized by disproportionate short stature and skeletal anomalies resulting from a defect in cartilage maturation.

The clinical indication for mirabegron is overactive bladder with symptoms of urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and urinary frequency and other storage symptoms in both men and women, and it is also indicated for urgency, urinary frequency and urge urinary incontinence associated with overactive bladder syndrome.

Tardive dyskinesia is a movement disorder caused by medications that block dopamine receptors, such as neuroleptics and anti-emetics, or through chronic use and high dosing of typical neuroleptics or centrally acting dopamine receptor blocking antiemetics.

Yes, single nucleotide polymorphisms within ultraconserved elements are associated with clinical outcomes and susceptibility to colorectal cancer. Studies identified associations between specific SNPs within these elements and both recurrence/survival in patients undergoing chemotherapy, as well as overall and site-specific CRC risk.

PANTHER-PSEP is a software tool used for predicting genetic variants that could cause human disease. It assesses variant pathogenicity by tracing the evolutionary history of amino acids to estimate how long a particular state has been preserved in ancestral proteins. This tool outperforms previous methods utilizing evolutionary conservation and even those incorporating multiple sources of information, and can be applied to predict variants in approximately 100 different species within the PANTHER database. It is freely available online and as a command-line tool.

MPE-seq is a new method used for the genome-wide analysis of chromatin structure. It characterizes chromatin through the digestion of nuclei with MPE-Fe(II) followed by massively parallel sequencing, much like micrococcal nuclease (MNase) but with some key differences in cleavage patterns. Unlike MNase, MPE-Fe(II) cleaves nuclear DNA with little sequence bias and can identify noncanonical chromatin structures important for gene regulation.

Bezlotoxumab neutralizes toxin B by binding to specific regions within the N-terminal half of the TcdB CROP domain, blocking its binding to mammalian cells. A single molecule of bezlotoxumab binds to two distinct epitopes within the CROP domain, partially occluding carbohydrate binding pockets. This passive immunotherapy prevents toxin-dependent symptoms during the early severe phase of disease, allowing the gut microbiota to recover without the effects of the toxin. Bezlotoxumab has been shown to decrease recurrent *C. difficile* infection by approximately 40%.

Yes, apremilast is demonstrated to be effective in treating moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis based on data from Phase I, II, and III clinical trials, with PASI 75 response rates between 28.8% and 33.1% after 16 weeks. It is also considered an effective option with a favorable safety profile and oral administration offering significant advantages.

Yes, the SLC24A5 gene locus is associated with skin pigmentation, and specific alleles of single-nucleotide polymorphisms within this gene—rs1426654 and rs1834640—associated with light skin pigmentation are found at a high frequency in one population. Additionally, variations in SLC24A5 were significantly associated with skin color in a study of Caucasians.
Arboviruses are transmitted to vertebrate hosts during blood feedings by mosquitoes, ticks, biting flies, mites, and nits.
Nasuia-ALF has the smallest bacterial genome yet sequenced, measuring 112 kb.

The classic triad of symptoms for pheochromocytoma includes headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis, though these may not always be present and can be accompanied by flushing, visual symptoms, flank pain, pallor, or new hypertension with orthostatic hypotension.

The five types of Cardiorenal syndrome are acute worsening of heart function leading to kidney injury, chronic abnormalities in heart function leading to kidney injury, acute worsening of kidney function leading to heart injury, chronic kidney disease leading to heart injury, and systemic conditions leading to simultaneous injury of both the heart and kidney.

A miR is a small, non-coding RNA that frequently exhibits deregulated expression in human cancer and functions as a post-transcriptional regulator by binding to the 3'UTRs of target mRNAs to repress gene expression.

No, circRNAs can function in translation, as they are able to bind to proteins and regulate translation, and may also regulate splicing. While many functions remain undetermined, they are not exclusively noncoding, and can participate in gene expression regulation beyond simply acting as competing endogenous RNAs or microRNA sponges.

The Sry circular RNA functions as a microRNA sponge, specifically targeting miR-138. Both linear and circular Sry transcripts can act as miRNA sponges, competing with other RNAs for miR-138 binding. This sponge effect is a general phenomenon achieved by circRNA formation and suggests a role for Sry in post-transcriptional regulation.

Uhl's anomaly is a rare congenital condition involving the partial or complete absence of right ventricular myocardium, or a hypoplasia of the right ventricular myocardium. It can present with severe right heart failure, sometimes even leading to foetal demise, and its cause is currently unknown. While outcomes are generally unfavorable, the duration of survival varies depending on the degree of right ventricular dysfunction.

No, autophagy is a self-eating process where damaged cell organelles and protein aggregates are removed, and it can also eliminate intracellular pathogens like viruses. However, the context also indicates viruses can manipulate autophagy to support their own replication, and input JEV particles are targeted *to* autophagosomes, not ingested *by* them. Additionally, the context details bacteria developing strategies to avoid or exploit autophagy, suggesting a reciprocal interaction rather than ingestion.

Aspirin is a potential chemopreventive agent that can reverse, suppress, or prevent the progression to invasive cancer, specifically colorectal cancer. Evidence from epidemiological studies and randomized controlled trials demonstrates that aspirin reduces the incidence of colorectal neoplasia, colorectal cancer, and associated mortality. Benefits are observed with daily aspirin use, though the ideal dose and duration are still being investigated, and it has shown promise in preventing cancers such as gastric and esophageal cancer as well.

The pre-replication complex in eukaryotes consists of Cdc6/Cdc18, MCM, ORC, Cdt1, Sap1/Girdin, Mcm10, Cdc45, Ipi1p, Ipi2p, and Ipi3p, with some components like Cdc6 and Mcm7 associating with origins during pre-RC establishment and maintenance.

Hypophosphatemic rickets can be inherited in autosomal recessive, autosomal dominant, or X-linked patterns, with some forms exhibiting autosomal recessive inheritance involving alterations in the sodium dependent phosphate cotransporter, others demonstrating autosomal dominant inheritance and potentially being affected by iron deficiency, and still others being X-linked and potentially dominant, as evidenced by familial cases involving the X chromosome.
Amanita phalloides is poisonous, as it causes poisoning requiring potential liver transplantation and has a derivative, antamanide, studied for its effects on cell death.

A 12-gene transcriptional signature, over-represented by STAT3-inducible genes, is identified in rheumatoid arthritis patients, particularly those who are anti-citrullinated peptide antibody-negative.

Burrows-Wheeler Alignment tool (BWA), Bowtie, and BWA-SW implement the Burrows-Wheeler Transform, and the Burrows-Wheeler transform is also utilized by other tools like CLAST and is referenced as a method that can be improved upon by NGS-specific compression schemes.

Yes, intraoperative radiotherapy is used for the treatment of glioblastoma multiforme, as it is described as a technique for both initial management of unresectable tumors and retreatment of recurrent tumors, and studies have examined its feasibility, morbidity, and effects on survival time in glioblastoma patients.
Pse-in-One is a web server used for generating various modes of pseudo components of DNA, RNA, and protein sequences.

A soluble factor, Rho GDP dissociation inhibitor (D4GDI), produced by certain cells inhibits growth of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* by enhancing production of TNF-α. D4GDI was concentrated at the site of disease in tuberculosis patients. Conversely, individuals with tuberculosis produced less D4GDI than those with latent tuberculosis infection, suggesting a link between reduced D4GDI and disease progression despite TNF-α’s role in combating the infection.
Mutations in ribosomal protein encoding genes are found in patients with Diamond-Blackfan anemia, and defects in the hematopoietic transcription factor GATA-1 can also cause the condition.

Human acrocentric chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, and 29 are involved in Robertsonian translocation, with chromosomes 14 and 15 being particularly associated with abnormal phenotypes and uniparental disomy.

Yes, tirilazad appears to improve overall outcome in aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage patients, particularly in males, as one trial demonstrated a dramatic reduction in mortality from 27% to 3% with a dosage of 6 mg/kg/day. However, benefits were predominantly shown in men, and further data were pending to assess its effectiveness in women.
Coilin is the main marker protein of Cajal bodies, used to identify these structures in various cell types.
Mutations in the CHS1/LYST gene cause Chédiak–Higashi Syndrome.
Fusarium graminearum can infect all cereal species, including wheat, and also causes disease on Arabidopsis thaliana.

Retroviruses, adenoviruses, and adeno-associated viruses are viral vectors utilized in gene therapy, with adeno-associated viral vectors currently being preferred due to their low pathogenicity and immunogenicity, and oncoretroviral vectors also being constructed for specific applications.

No. Dexamethasone and other glucocorticoids should be avoided in the treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage, and current practices of using dexamethasone for this condition should be reconsidered due to a lack of beneficial effect and evidence of significant harmful effects.

Disruption of a TAD boundary can lead to aberrant gene expression by exposing genes to inappropriate regulatory elements.
