RNA polymerase binds to a minimal promoter region, specifically the TATA box, to initiate transcription.

Yes, PRP-40 is a central regulator of microexon splicing and is required for the inclusion of alternatively spliced microexons, particularly in the nervous system. It is specifically required for microexons surrounded by conventionally sized introns, and its ortholog in mouse neuroblastoma cells dysregulates microexons when knocked down.
Loss of independent ambulation in Duchenne muscular dystrophy typically occurs between the ages of 7 and 13 years, or around 9 to 11 years, and historically by age 12.

Yes, ocrelizumab is highly efficacious in primary progressive multiple sclerosis and is the only disease-modifying therapy approved for this condition. It was also approved as the first drug for people with early primary progressive multiple sclerosis by the European Medicines Agency.

Indocyanine green is a fluorescent dye utilized in surgery as a guide and for imaging vascular structures. It’s applied to assess vascularization, particularly in urologic and colorectal procedures, and can help prevent postoperative complications like suture dehiscence. Additionally, ICG can be incorporated into emulsions to prolong its presence in the bloodstream and enhance its absorbing capacity during procedures like laser-induced photocoagulation.
Alternative splicing generates multiple transcripts from the same gene, and is a key mechanism regulating gene expression that can increase proteome diversity.

No, silencing of SRRM4 promotes tumor growth across cancers. The text indicates that SRRM4 is silenced in tumors and this silencing is favorable for tumor growth, correlating with increased mitotic gene expression and inhibiting proliferation when SRRM4 is upregulated.

The median life expectancy for Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients is 22.0 years, though recent patients born after 1990 have a median life expectancy exceeding this value and some may live to be over 30 years of age.

Yes, daridorexant improves sleep quality and is superior to placebo in reducing wake time after sleep onset, latency to persistent sleep, and increasing total sleep time. It is a novel dual orexin receptor antagonist used in treating insomnia disorder.
Erythroferrone is produced by erythroblasts in response to erythropoietin and also by osteoblasts independently of erythropoietin.

The Mediator complex is an essential co-regulator of RNA polymerase II, required for transcription in all eukaryotes. It functions in gene regulation by linking gene-specific transcription factors to RNA polymerase II, impacting nearly all stages of mRNA synthesis—including initiation, elongation, and termination. Mediator transduces signals from transcription activators to the transcription machinery, and can reorganize its structure to fit cellular needs, ultimately controlling gene expression and influencing development or contributing to diseases like cancer.
FBDD stands for fragment-based drug discovery.

Yes, deucravacitinib is effective for psoriasis. Studies demonstrate significant improvements in psoriasis-related clinical signs and symptoms, as measured by reductions in Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) scores, and these improvements are maintained over time—up to two years in some cases. Additionally, it improves quality of life, with many patients achieving considerable improvement even without complete skin clearance.

Orexin/hypocretin neurons regulate sleep/wake states, feeding behavior, energy homeostasis, reward systems, cognition, mood, arousal, and contribute to the regulation of multiple homeostatic and behavioral processes, including arousal state switching, and can also rapidly change their firing in response to external stimuli or self-paced exploration.

Yes, the methylation status of introns is correlated with their expression levels in mRNA, and hypomethylation of introns is associated with higher levels of intron expression. Additionally, intron methylation levels are inversely correlated with intron retention in mRNA.

Keytruda was originally approved by the FDA for the treatment of metastatic non-small cell lung cancer in patients whose tumors express programmed death-ligand 1, metastatic melanoma, recurrent or metastatic head and neck cancer, refractory Hodgkin lymphoma, and urothelial carcinoma, all of which carry positive programmed death-1/programmed death-ligand 1 biomarkers, and later expanded to include treatment of triple-negative breast cancer when used in combination with chemotherapy.

The Achard-Thiers syndrome presents with both overt diabetes and marked hirsutism. Patients with this syndrome often experience obesity, hypertension, and angiopathy. Investigations reveal hypercorticism and depressed insulin activity, though increased steroiduria can also be observed in diabetic women without hirsutism, making definitive separation from common diabetes uncertain.

No, luminopsins are fusion proteins of luciferase and opsin. Several instances describe luminopsins as being created by fusing luciferase to an opsin, enabling both chemogenetic and optogenetic manipulation of neuronal activity. One instance specifically mentions fusion of Gaussia luciferase with channelrhodopsin to create luminopsins.

Coactivators are diverse and multifunctional proteins that stimulate transcription downstream of DNA-binding activators. They are recruited to DNA by nuclear receptors and function as adaptors in signaling pathways transmitting transcriptional responses to the basal transcriptional machinery. Some coactivators are essential for receptor-mediated transcriptional activation and require interaction with transcription factors like TEAD proteins to activate transcription. These proteins dynamically modulate their molecular properties through posttranslational modifications, influencing gene expression and physiological processes.

PROTACs are engineered, bifunctional small molecules that recruit a target protein and an E3 ubiquitin ligase to trigger proteasomal degradation of the target protein via the ubiquitin-proteasome system.
The ALBI grade includes assessments of albumin and bilirubin levels, and a modified version, mALBI, is also used to evaluate hepatic functional reserve.

Prednisone, cyclophosphamide, recombinant feline interferon, glucocorticoids, antibiotics, Feliserin, doxycycline, and the antiviral nucleoside analogue GS-441524 are used to treat FIP in cats, although many of these treatments have not been proven effective in controlled studies and historically, most affected cats succumb to the disease despite intervention.

Yes, there are ways to clinically distinguish COVID-19 from other respiratory illnesses. Several sources indicate the difficulty in differentiation but also highlight specific features. Fever is predicted to initiate COVID-19, while cough initiates influenza. Additionally, anosmia and dysgeusia are independently associated with COVID-19, and gastrointestinal symptoms, dry cough, and specific immune responses differ between the diseases. Platelet count and indices also show statistically significant differences between COVID-19 and influenza pneumonia.
Paclitaxel, docetaxel, doxorubicin, and epirubicin have been approved to treat triple-negative breast cancer and, in some cases, are used in treating HER2-positive breast cancer.
Talquetamab was developed for treatment of relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma and is a bispecific therapeutic with a marketing application submission potentially occurring by the end of 2023.

Feline spongiform encephalopathy (FSE) is a transmissible spongiform encephalopathy affecting domestic cats and other feline species, believed to result from consuming food contaminated by the agent of bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE). It is characterized by the accumulation of an abnormal prion protein (PrP(sc)) in the central and, in some cases, peripheral nervous systems, as well as other tissues like the spleen, kidney, and adrenal gland.  Clinically, FSE presents as a progressive and fatal neurological syndrome, and can potentially be transmitted maternally, with cases reported in both Europe and Great Britain. Studies have shown similarities between the FSE agent and the cattle BSE agent, suggesting they may harbor the same strain.

Yes, untranslated regions can regulate gene expression by influencing mRNA stability, translational efficiency, and post-transcriptional processes like mRNA processing, transport, and localization. They also harbor recognition sites for RNA-binding proteins and microRNA target sites, and differential use of alternative UTRs can control protein expression in a tissue-specific manner.

No. The provided information details associations between alternative splicing and myotonic dystrophy, developmental stages, neurodegeneration, and chromatin regulation, but does not mention any connection to heart disease. While myotonic dystrophy is a muscular dystrophy and affects muscle tissue, the context does not extend this to specifically implicate heart disease.

No, intepirdine did not produce statistical improvement over placebo on cognition or activities of daily living in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer’s disease patients, and larger trials have failed to demonstrate a statistically significant impact on cognition when used alongside cholinesterase inhibitors. While it was considered a promising treatment strategy, current phase-3 trials have not been positive.
Hepatocytes are usually targeted with a GalNAc-conjugated antisense oligonucleotide due to the selective uptake facilitated by the asialoglycoprotein receptor present on these liver cells.

Rebound COVID-19 is a clinical association with SARS-CoV-2 infection that occurs five to eight days after stopping antiviral treatment. It can involve a recurrence of COVID-19 infections or symptoms. This phenomenon has been observed after treatment with both Paxlovid and Molnupiravir, and can also occur in untreated individuals, though high-level viral and symptom rebound without antiviral treatment is rare.

Zanubrutinib is approved in the United States for treating adults with mantle cell lymphoma who have received at least one prior therapy, for adults with Waldenström macroglobulinemia, and for adults with relapsed or refractory marginal zone lymphoma who have received at least one anti-CD20-based therapy.

Yes, rilonacept is effective for pericarditis. It is associated with a lower risk of recurrence, more persistent clinical response, and higher amounts of days with no or minimal pericarditis symptoms compared to placebo. It also leads to rapid resolution of pericarditis episodes and decreases pain and inflammation, as evidenced by reductions in numeric rating scales and C-reactive protein levels. Furthermore, it allows patients to potentially transition to rilonacept monotherapy and has demonstrated efficacy in both symptomatic and corticosteroid-dependent patients.

The 1p19q co-deletion is associated with primary brain tumors of oligodendroglial histology, extraventricular neurocytomas, and gliomas, and can help distinguish oligodendrogliomas from other glial neoplasms.
Xist encodes a non-coding RNA that functions as the primary switch for X-inactivation, coating the X chromosome from which it is transcribed.

Microexons are small sized exons, measuring up to 51 base pairs, though some are as short as 27 nucleotides or even 3-27 nucleotides long. They undergo extensive alternative splicing in cells like neurons, microglia, embryonic stem cells, and cancer cells, resulting in cell type-specific protein isoforms. Because of their small size, they present a unique challenge to the splicing machinery and often require specialized RNA binding proteins for their inclusion in mRNA.

Yes, tebentafusp has demonstrated improved overall survival and progression-free survival in patients with metastatic uveal melanoma, and is now approved as a first-line therapy for those with the HLA-A*02:01 antigen. It is considered a promising advancement and has transformed the treatment paradigm for this disease.

The Fenton reaction is a reaction between iron and hydrogen peroxide. This reaction generates hydroxyl radicals and can also produce higher oxidation states of iron, and is capable of oxidizing many substrates while causing biological damage. It is a complex reaction closely involved in programmed cell death called ferroptosis, and has applications in fields like cancer treatment and sterilization. Conditions like pH and ligand concentrations can influence the yield of hydroxyl radicals produced by the Fenton reaction.

Tryptophan absorption defects are postulated as the underlying pathology of Blue Diaper Syndrome, and phenylalanine and tyrosine dietary restrictions can alleviate symptoms in a related metabolic disorder, alkaptonuria.

SOX9 is a transcription factor belonging to the HMG domain superfamily and plays a role in various developmental pathways in vertebrates. It is important for homeostasis in the intestinal epithelium and has been linked to both tumor suppression and oncogenic behavior in colorectal cancers, depending on its level of expression. Additionally, SOX9 is involved in the specification and differentiation of cells, including oligodendrocytes, thyroid follicular cells, and the maintenance of the notochord during development. Its function can be modulated by interactions between its dimerization and transactivation domains.

Ubiquitination regulates protein half-lives, influences cell cycle progression, differentiation, antigen presentation, retrovirus assembly, apoptosis, signal transduction, transcriptional activation, biological clocks, receptor downregulation and endocytosis, and plays roles in gene transcription, gene repression, DNA damage and repair, as well as being implicated in neurological disorders like epilepsy.
The primary endpoints of the phase I/II double-blind, comparator-controlled trial of RT001 in Friedreich's ataxia patients were safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic analysis.
Friedreich's ataxia is caused by a deficiency of frataxin protein or by pathological expansion of GAA repeats in the first intron of the X25 gene on chromosome 9, leading to frataxin deficiency.

Serpentine supravenous hyperpigmentation can be caused by peripheral infusion of chemotherapy agents such as 5-fluorouracil, docetaxel, vinorelbine, fotemustine, alkylating agents, antibiotics, anti-microtubules, proteasome-inhibitors, and minocycline, and may also be associated with conditions like collagen vascular diseases, HIV infection, or potentially be a normal racial variant in dark-skinned individuals.

Alpha-synuclein (α-syn) aggregation contributes to neuronal cell death in Parkinson’s disease, often forming Lewy bodies.  Increased phosphorylation of α-syn at serine 129 is linked to the formation of these Lewy bodies and dopaminergic neurodegeneration. Furthermore, α-syn can inhibit neurotrophic signaling, like BDNF/TrkB, and interact synergistically with neuroinflammation and plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 to promote this cell death.  Misfolding of α-syn also triggers toxicity and eventual cell death, though some proteins can offer protection against this cytotoxicity.

Positive regulators of cell cycle progression, such as cyclin-dependent kinases and their cyclin partners, can induce cell cycle progression and contribute to cancer when abnormally expressed or activated.
Hereditary angioedema is a rare disease caused by mutations.
Friedreich's ataxia usually occurs before the age of 25, though late-onset forms can occur after 25 and very-late-onset forms after 40 years of age.
The World Hip Trauma Evaluation (WHiTE) trial focused on osteoporotic hip fractures and displaced intracapsular hip fractures.

Yes, FND is described as a neuropsychiatric illness and is associated with psychological factors such as stress, trauma, and interpersonal difficulties. It’s characterized by neurological symptoms without a structural neurological cause, and shared mechanisms exist between FND and placebo/nocebo effects which involve expectations and learning – suggesting a connection between mind and body symptoms. Psychological interventions like psychotherapy are also emerging as evidence-based treatments for FND.

No, accessible chromatin is not necessarily indicative of active enhancers. While open chromatin is widely used to infer enhancer activity, the relationship is more nuanced than simply open versus closed. Active enhancers display a *higher degree* of accessibility than primed or repressed states, but accessibility alone is not a specific indicator of activity, and bidirectional transcription initiation is a feature of accessible chromatin that is not specific to enhancers. Furthermore, enhancers can be masked by a repressive chromatin signature and remain inaccessible.
RTA 408 is also known as omaveloxolone, an FDA-approved drug.

Spinal Muscular Atrophy is caused by deficiency of the essential Survival Motor Neuron (SMN) protein due to deletions or mutations in the gene responsible for it, or by exon deletions or loss-of-function mutations.

No, prasinezumab therapy had no meaningful effect on global or imaging measures of Parkinson's disease progression when compared to placebo. It was associated with infusion reactions in some participants.

Luminopsins are fusion proteins created by combining luciferase and opsin, enabling neuronal circuit interrogation using either physical or biological light sources. These tools allow for genetically encoded manipulation of neuronal activity through both chemogenetic and optogenetic approaches, offering control of neuronal activity with less invasive methods. Different iterations of luminopsins exist, some designed to excite neurons and others to inhibit them, and they have demonstrated effectiveness in both *in vitro* and *in vivo* applications, even promoting axon regeneration after nerve injury. They can be activated by external light or a luciferase substrate like coelenterazine.

The hallmarks of cancer include sustaining proliferative signaling, evading growth suppressors, resisting cell death, enabling replicative immortality, inducing angiogenesis, activating invasion and metastasis, genomic instability, tumor-promoting inflammation, deregulation of cellular energy metabolism, evading immune destruction, insensitivity to negative growth regulation, lack of senescence, and genome elasticity.
The phase of the clinical trial involving mepolizumab for eosinophilic oesophagitis in ten adult patients in Switzerland was phase I/II.
Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy is related to genes encoding proteins associated with the nuclear envelope, specifically emerin, lamins A and C, EMD, and LMNA.

No, AAVS3, specifically FLT180a (verbrinacogene setparvovec), was developed for hemophilia B to normalize factor IX levels in patients with the condition. The provided information details its use in a phase 1-2 trial for hemophilia B patients and does not mention any application to hemophilia A.
Luciferase substrates include D-luciferin, CycLuc1, and 6'-aminoluciferin, with D-luciferin being a natural substrate and CycLuc1 a synthetic analog.

Master regulatory genes trigger major developmental decisions and act at the top of regulatory hierarchies, determining complete developmental programs. These genes often encode transcription factors and regulate gene expression through mechanisms like chromatin modification. They can override existing differentiation programs and are crucial in both organism development and processes like carcinogenesis, potentially serving as therapeutic targets. Identifying these genes is important for understanding disease and developing effective treatments.
RT001 has been evaluated in a phase I/II double-blind, comparator-controlled trial against Friedreich's ataxia.

The known inflammatory myopathies include dermatomyositis, immune-mediated necrotising myopathy, sporadic inclusion-body myositis, overlap myositis—including antisynthetase syndrome—and polymyositis, with additional classifications encompassing autoimmune necrotizing myopathies and consideration of overlap syndromes associated with connective tissue diseases like scleroderma or systemic lupus erythematosus.
L9LS was developed for the prevention and elimination of malaria, a disease that causes illness and death among infants and young children.

Yes, iron regulates oligodendrocyte maturation. Several sources indicate iron is essential for oligodendrocyte development and maturation, with disruptions in iron handling or availability leading to delays or loss of oligodendrocytes and impaired myelination. Upregulation of transferrin receptor and ferritin-H during oligodendrocyte maturation also implies iron’s essential role in this process. Additionally, astrocytic H-ferritin expression is necessary for proper oligodendrocyte development and myelination.

Genome-wide association studies are a powerful tool used to examine the human genome and detect genetic risk factors for diseases. These studies assay a dense set of single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) across the whole genome and relate them to various diseases and traits. GWAS identify common genetic variants associated with complex disorders and can reveal the genetic architecture of diseases, opening opportunities for treatment and prevention. They are an attractive tool for genetic testing due to their comprehensive coverage and comparatively low cost.
PROTACs stands for Proteolysis Targeting Chimeras, which are engineered techniques for targeted protein degradation and represent a novel class of heterobifunctional molecules.
The incidence of Leigh syndrome is reported as 1 in 77,000 live births worldwide and also as 1 in 40,000 live births.
The Trauma Triad of Death is characterized by hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy, and represents a physiologic limit reached during trauma resuscitation, particularly in the operating room.

Glossectomy is a procedure involving the removal of all or part of the tongue. It is considered a mutilating surgery in head and neck procedures, altering vital functions like mastication, language, and swallowing. This procedure can be performed in both primary and salvage settings for advanced tongue cancer, and may sometimes be combined with other surgeries like laryngectomy, though this is controversial. Rehabilitation after a total glossectomy often involves prosthetic devices to recreate normalcy and balance in the oral cavity.

Examples of epigenetic modifications include DNA methylation, histone modifications, and small non-coding RNAs, and these events can independently or collaboratively regulate cellular mechanisms like transcription, DNA repair, and DNA replication.
Zanubrutinib inhibits Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK), a cytoplasmic protein-tyrosine kinase essential for the differentiation and survival of B-lineage cells.

Friedreich's ataxia is categorized into typical-onset, late-onset (occurring after 25 years), very-late-onset (occurring after 40 years), and delayed-onset, which encompasses both late- and very-late-onset forms, and can even present after 60 years of age.

The FAKTION trial investigated whether adding capivasertib to fulvestrant improved progression-free survival, and participants received either capivasertib 400 mg or a matching placebo alongside fulvestrant 500 mg.

Gene knock-down is a technology used to study gene function and can potentially provide therapy for viral diseases. It works by interfering with gene expression, often utilizing antisense molecules like morpholino phosphorodiamidate oligonucleotides to reduce the level of specific gene products. This can be achieved through microinjection or siRNA-mediated reduction of protein expression, and the effects are often dose-dependent, with optimal concentrations minimizing toxicity while still disrupting gene expression. In some cases, knocking down multiple genes simultaneously can have synergistic effects.

The transition from precursor mRNA to mature mRNA involves capping, splicing, 3' end processing with polyadenylation—requiring sequences distal to the poly(A) addition site for proper cleavage—and packaging into an mRNA ribonucleoprotein complex before export to the cytoplasm for translation, all carried out by dedicated molecular machines and integrated with transcription.
Apretude, also known as cabotegravir extended-release injectable suspension, is used for HIV-1 pre-exposure prophylaxis.

Spinal Muscular Atrophy includes types I, II, and III, which vary in age of onset, diagnostic age, and diagnostic delay, and also encompasses proximal spinal muscular atrophy characterized by motor neuron loss and skeletal muscle atrophy.
Ublituximab is being evaluated for the treatment of relapsing multiple sclerosis.

Palmar erythema commonly presents as symmetric, blanchable, slightly warm, nonscaling erythema, most frequently involving the thenar and hypothenar eminences of the palmar surface and is sometimes referred to as “liver palms.”

Yes, epigenetic changes can be heritable. Several sources indicate that these changes are transmitted across generations, creating a “cellular memory” and impacting evolution, sometimes even before genetic changes occur. These heritable epigenetic changes can alter wave patterns, affect rates of fitness increase, and lead to cumulative micro-evolutionary changes.

Trichotillomania requires a comprehensive and interdisciplinary treatment plan, and physicians should approach patients with empathy. Behavioral therapy and pharmacotherapy, including tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and glutamate-modulating agents like N-acetylcysteine, can be utilized. Additionally, alternative therapies such as yoga, aerobic exercise, acupuncture, biofeedback, and hypnosis show promise, and inositol is being investigated as a potential treatment option.

Oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy is caused by mutations in the polyadenylate binding protein nuclear 1 (PABPN1) gene or by short (GCG) repeat expansions in the polyadenylation binding protein 2 (PABP2) gene, with autosomal dominant inheritance being typical and a rare autosomal recessive form also existing.
EDP-938 can be used to treat respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection, which causes substantial morbidity and mortality among infants, older adults, and immunocompromised adults.
Durvalumab is the generic name for Imfinzi.
Negative regulators of the cell cycle that, when targeted for inactivation, can contribute to cancer are tumor suppressor genes.

Keytruda was previously approved for treatment of metastatic melanoma, among other malignancies, carrying positive programmed death-1/programmed death-ligand 1 biomarkers, and indications for Keytruda significantly expanded by 2017.
Oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy has a world-wide incidence but is more common in the French Canadian population.
The Mackler triad consists of vomiting, severe chest pain, and subcutaneous cervical emphysema.

Tastin is a proline-rich cytoplasmic protein initially identified as an accessory protein for trophinin, a cell adhesion molecule involved in early embryo implantation. It associates with the microtubular cytoskeleton and interacts with proteins like Tctex-1 and dynein intermediate chain p150(Glued). Furthermore, tastin is required for bipolar spindle assembly and centrosome integrity during mitosis, peaking in levels during the G(2)/M phase.

No, epigenetic changes do not alter the DNA sequence. They are heritable changes in gene expression patterns that occur without altering the DNA sequence itself, and instead modify how DNA sequences are read. These modifications alter gene expression without changing the underlying genetic code.

Ruconest is a new drug developed for the relief of symptoms in patients with angioedema due to C1-inhibitor deficiency. It is a recombinant human C1 esterase inhibitor that blocks the actions of enzymes in the bradykinin-forming pathway. Clinical trials established 50 U/kg as the recommended dose, and the drug received marketing authorization from the EMA.

Motor neuron diseases include amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, progressive bulbar palsy, progressive muscular atrophy, primary lateral sclerosis, spinal muscular atrophy, spinobulbar muscular atrophy, hereditary motor neuropathies, and various forms of lower motor neuron syndromes, with some overlap existing between these conditions and others like Charcot’s disease and Kennedy’s disease.
Donidalorsen was tested for hereditary angioedema.

Therapeutic plasma exchange is used in the treatment of neurological, hematological, renal, and autoimmune diseases, including inflammatory polyneuropathy, acquired demyelinating diseases of the central nervous system, autoimmune encephalitis, paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis, autoimmune haematological diseases, rheumatic diseases, myasthenia gravis, macrophage activation syndrome, acute and acute on chronic liver failure, and conditions with known or suspected immune pathogenesis.
The genes comprising the lactose uptake pathway are collectively called the lac operon, and their transcription leads to the production of proteins that import and metabolize lactose.
Alternative microexon splicing is associated with metastasis in colorectal cancer and lung cancer.

Leigh syndrome can be maternally inherited due to mutations in mitochondrial DNA, specifically the T8993G mutation or the G10254A substitution in the ND3 gene, and it can also be X-linked as seen in PDHA1 deficiency or caused by mutations in nuclear DNA-encoded genes like NDUFS4.

Zinner syndrome features a triad of seminal vesicle cysts, ipsilateral ejaculatory duct obstruction, and unilateral renal agenesis, and is associated with symptoms like haematuria, dysuria, perineal pain, infertility, painful ejaculation, and potentially fever and oliguria, stemming from an anomaly in the development of the Wolffian duct or mesonephric ducts.

A regulon is a collection of co-regulated genes, such as the 48 genes composing the DosR regulon. This regulon is essential for Mycobacterium tuberculosis persistence and survival during dormancy, particularly in lengthy hypoxia. The DosR regulon is induced by conditions inhibiting aerobic respiration and plays a crucial role in metabolic homeostasis and recovery from nonrespiring dormancy.
Yes, a case of skin picking developed after methylphenidate therapy for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, suggesting a potential link between the two.

Spinal-bulbar muscular atrophy is caused by expansion of a trinucleotide CAG repeat, which encodes the polyglutamine tract, in the first exon of the androgen receptor gene, or by a missense mutation in UBA1.
Tezepelumab can be used to treat severe asthma, even in patients with uncontrolled disease despite other treatments, and regardless of blood eosinophil counts.

No, enhancers are not necessarily close to the genes they act on. The entanglement structure of chromatin allows positions far apart in the sequence to have the opportunity to contact each other, and enhancers can exert their effects over tens of kilobases of DNA by looping away intervening sequences to come into close proximity with target promoters.
No. The provided information discusses spinal muscular atrophy types I, II, and III, and references non-ambulant types 2 and 3, but does not mention any other types beyond these classifications.

Zanidatamab is a bispecific antibody that targets two non-overlapping domains of HER2. It is designed for the treatment of locally advanced or metastatic cancers expressing or amplifying HER2. Studies evaluated the safety and anti-tumor activity of zanidatamab across a range of solid tumors, with a recommended dose of 20 mg/kg every 2 weeks identified through clinical trials.

SOX10 is a critical transcription factor for several cell lineages, including neural crest stem cells, peripheral glial cells, and melanocytes. It functions as an activator of the MITF promoter and cooperates with other transcription factors to activate gene expression, such as key myelin genes in Schwann cells. Furthermore, SOX10 directly regulates genes involved in development and can inhibit glial cell differentiation through regulation of SOX6. Mutations in SOX10 are associated with several neurocristopathies and can impact melanocyte development.

Friedreich's ataxia is an autosomal recessive neurodegenerative disease caused by expansions of a GAA trinucleotide repeat in the STM7/X25 gene. It presents as a progressive ataxia and can resemble vitamin E deficiency ataxia, though molecular analysis can differentiate between the two. The disease’s phenotypic spectrum is broad, and its progression may be influenced by the number of GAA repeats, with smaller repeats correlating to earlier onset and faster progression.

No, no beneficial treatment effects were recorded in the study of tilavonemab for progressive supranuclear palsy. The study did not provide evidence of efficacy, despite investigating the safety and efficacy of the treatment.

Homeotic transformation involves the change of one body part into the likeness of something else. It can occur when body parts normally found in one domain are replaced by those found elsewhere, and this is seen in both model organisms and human developmental defects. This transformation may be induced by processes like the autotransplantation of tissue or alterations in gene expression, resulting in structures with combined features or altered identities along an axis.

Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) is a life-threatening, autosomal recessive disease characterized by motor neuron loss and subsequent muscle weakness and atrophy. It is a genetic disorder of motor neurons and the most common monogenic cause of infant mortality, often resulting from decreased levels of the survival of motor neuron (SMN) protein. The disease presents with a spectrum of severity, ranging from severe infantile-onset forms to milder, late-onset types, and may mimic other neurological conditions.

Hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obesity, increased C-reactive protein, low percutaneous oxygen saturation, reduced lymphocyte counts, kidney injury, being overweight, obstructive sleep apnoea, genetically predicted increases in body mass index and lifetime smoking, and increased inflammation, myocardial injury, and kidney injury indicators are all associated with severe COVID-19.

No. Multiple studies have analyzed breast milk samples from mothers with COVID-19 and have generally found either no detectable SARS-CoV-2 RNA or very rare instances of positivity, without confirmed transmission to infants. While some studies identified a small number of positive breast milk samples, these were infrequent and did not consistently correlate with infant infection, and current evidence does not support transmission through breast milk.

Chromatin is tightly packed into a hierarchically ordered structure called chromatin, consisting of nucleosomes which are a short piece of DNA wrapped around a core of eight histone proteins, and further folded into higher-order structures through various mechanisms.

No, there is not currently an applicable treatment option for Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy, though several drug trials are continuing, including those focused on DUX4 and TGF-ß inhibition. Some studies are also investigating nutraceuticals and phytochemicals as potential treatment strategies, and gene repair strategies are being explored, but relief has not yet been provided. Cocktail therapy with vitamins has been used to improve muscle metabolism in some cases.

Enhancers function as regulatory elements that integrate signals to control gene expression, and mutations in these elements can misregulate growth-related genes in cancer. These misregulations can lead to the formation of tumor-specific super-enhancers and establish a state of uncontrolled cell proliferation. Targeting super-enhancers, which drive oncogenic programs, shows promise in reducing tumor growth and proliferation, suggesting they are potential therapeutic targets.

Currently used non-pharmacological measures for managing procedural pain in neonates include breastfeeding, swaddling, sweet solutions like sucrose and glucose, stroking of the skin, non-nutritive sucking, and physical methods—though parents often require training to effectively implement these strategies—as well as utilizing pain assessment scales such as COMFORT, PIPP-R, NIPS, N-PASS, Bernese Pain Scale for Neonates (BPSN), and Premature Infant Pain Profile (PIPP).

No, while methionine is the universal translation start, the first methionine is removed from most mature proteins. Additionally, protein synthesis can be initiated with other amino acids under certain conditions, such as valine and glutamine in specific systems and with modified codons.

Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is characterized by facial weakness, scapular winging, and abdominal weakness with a positive Beevor sign, and it results from DUX4 protein expression when the locus on chromosome 4q35 is hypomethylated, presenting as a complex and heterogeneous disease with both adult- and pediatric-onset forms and an autosomal dominant mode of transmission.

MITF, SOX10, TFAP2A, and YY1 are essential transcription factors for the melanocyte lineage, alongside ZEB2 which regulates MITF levels to control melanocyte differentiation, and TFAP2B which specifies an embryonic melanocyte stem cell population.

FSHD2 is caused by mutations in the genes SMCHD1, DNMT3B, and LRIF1, often in combination with a normal-sized D4Z4 array on a chromosome 4 haplotype permissive for DUX4 expression, leading to epigenetic derepression of DUX4 in skeletal muscle cells.

Spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA) is an X-linked adult-onset progressive neuromuscular disease affecting spinal and bulbar motor neurons and skeletal muscles. It is a hereditary motor neuron disease caused by an expansion of a CAG trinucleotide repeat, or a polyglutamine tract, within the androgen receptor (AR) gene. This expansion leads to nuclear accumulation of the mutant AR, resulting in loss of lower motor neurons and progressive muscle weakness and atrophy. The disease primarily occurs in adult males, though females carrying the mutant gene can also exhibit symptoms.

Common symptoms in female carriers of Duchenne muscular dystrophy include muscle weakness, increased serum creatine kinase levels, compensatory movements, longer timed performance on functional tasks, pseudohypertrophy of calf muscles, asymmetrical muscle weakness, difficulty running, proximal muscle weakness, and cardiomyopathy, while some may also experience learning disabilities.

Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease encompasses demyelinating CMT1, axonal CMT2, intermediate CMT, and can be further divided into types 1A, 1B, and dominant intermediate forms like DI-CMTA, DI-CMTB, and DI-CMTC, with inheritance patterns including autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, and X-linked.

Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy type 1 is caused by a pathogenic contraction of the D4Z4 repeat array on chromosome 4q, leading to inappropriate expression of the transcription factor DUX4 in skeletal muscle tissue.

Kearns-Sayre syndrome is a rare mitochondrial disease often characterized by a triad of progressive external ophthalmoplegia, pigmentary retinopathy, and heart block. It is a multisystem disorder that typically appears in early childhood, though onset can occur in young adults. A large-scale mitochondrial DNA deletion is present in most cases, and cardiac involvement is a poor prognostic factor, potentially leading to cardiac failure.

Golodirsen is a drug used to treat Duchenne muscular dystrophy, a genetic disorder caused by loss-of-function mutations in the dystrophin gene. It functions as an antisense oligonucleotide that induces therapeutic exon skipping during pre-mRNA processing, restoring the reading frame of the dystrophin transcript. This process results in the production of truncated but partially functional dystrophin, which can potentially slow disease progression.

Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy is a rare, genetically heterogeneous disorder characterized by early contractures and progressive muscle weakness. It affects both skeletal and cardiac muscles, often leading to muscular atrophy and cardiomyopathy with conduction defects. Cardiac involvement, which can be life threatening, may present as dilated cardiomyopathy, ventricular arrhythmia, or atrio-ventricular block, and sometimes is the first symptom observed. The disease can be X-linked or autosomal and involves proteins associated with the nuclear envelope.

No, 12 months of treatment with losartan was not effective in reducing the rate of brain atrophy in individuals with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease. The difference in total volume reduction between the losartan and placebo groups was not statistically significant.

CHARMS is a checklist created to provide methodological appraisals of predictive models through systematic reviews, based on scientific evidence, and is used for critical appraisal and data extraction for systematic reviews of prediction modeling studies.

CCCTC-binding factor, the Argonaute protein AGO1, and members of the heterochromatin protein 1 family have been implicated in the regulation of splicing associated with chromatin and the elongation of RNA polymerase II in relation to alternative splicing patterns, and splicing factors that differentially express and alternatively splice could result in global alternative splicing.

Duchenne muscular dystrophy results from mutations in the dystrophin gene leading to the absence of the full-length dystrophin protein, and deletions in this gene are proven in approximately 70% of affected boys, while other mutations include expanded trinucleotide repeats in the myotonic dystrophy protein kinase gene and contractions in the subtelomeric region of chromosome 1 associated with facioscapulohumeral dystrophy.
Litifilimab targets blood dendritic cell antigen 2 (BDCA2), which is exclusively expressed on plasmacytoid dendritic cells.

AUROC, or area under the receiver operating characteristics curve, is a score used to evaluate the performance of predictive models, with higher values indicating better predictive ability, as demonstrated in tasks like risk prediction of heart failure, pancreatic cancer, spinal surgeries, annualized bleeding rate, in-hospital mortality, and detection of VUR.

Yes, knockdown of the orthologous PRPF40A in mouse neuroblastoma cells causes widespread dysregulation of microexons but not conventionally sized exons, and PRP-40 regulation of neuronal microexons is therefore a widely conserved phenomenon.

Yes, casimersen is effective for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in patients with a specific mutation. It received its first approval based on an observed increase in dystrophin production in skeletal muscle and is designed to allow for production of an internally truncated but functional dystrophin protein. It is used for patients who have a confirmed mutation of the DMD gene that is amenable to exon 45 skipping.

Tirzepatide targets both the glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptors. It functions as a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, demonstrating a greater engagement with the GIP receptor than the GLP-1 receptor. This dual action contributes to improved glycemic control, insulin sensitivity, weight reduction, and enhanced lipid metabolism.

Yes, medical hydrology and spa therapy appear to be related concepts. One individual worked to improve the status of spa medicine by recasting it as medical hydrology, and spa therapy is described as an integral part of treatment for burn scars and potentially influencing cortisol levels for stress management.

CYP1A2, a gene responsible for most caffeine metabolism, modifies how coffee consumption impacts appetite and BMI. Individuals who are rapid caffeine metabolizers experience lower appetite perception and BMI with coffee consumption, while slow/intermediate metabolizers do not show the same effects. Coffee consumption is also linked to lower energy and dietary fat intake, as well as circulating asprosin levels, and this relationship differs based on CYP1A2 genotype.

Yes, eteplirsen received accelerated approval from the US FDA for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy and has demonstrated increases in dystrophin levels, a decrease in the rate of decline in ambulation, and improvements in pulmonary function in some patients. While its therapeutic effect remains controversial and evidence is insufficient for some, it is designed to skip exon 51, restore the reading frame, and induce production of dystrophin protein.

Darolutamide is a medicine used to treat men with prostate cancer. It is used for both nonmetastatic and metastatic, hormone-sensitive prostate cancer. In trials, combining darolutamide with other treatments increased survival and lowered the risk of death, and it helped treat patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer better than placebo. It works as an androgen receptor antagonist and has shown to prolong metastasis-free survival.

The main clinical features of small-fiber neuropathy include pain, sensory disturbances, or autonomic dysfunction, and can manifest as burning pains, numbness, tingling, pins and needles, electric discharges, allodynia, vasomotor symptoms, and hyperhidrosis, alongside potentially intense squeezing and pressure sensations, frequent dynamic mechanical allodynia, and restless leg syndrome with pain aggravated at rest that improves with movement.

Keytruda was first approved by the FDA on October 24, 2016, for the treatment of patients with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer whose tumors express programmed death-ligand 1, and indications for its use significantly expanded by May 23, 2017.

Gene therapies under investigation for Duchenne muscular dystrophy include gene replacement with adeno-associated virus, exon skipping with antisense oligonucleotides, mutation suppression with compounds that read through stop codon mutations, gene therapy using adeno-associated viral, lentiviral and adenoviral vectors and nonviral vectors such as plasmid DNA, cell-based therapies, surrogate genes, and oligonucleotide-mediated gene repair using chimeraplasts.

No, downregulation of FNDC5 increases steatosis and insulin resistance, and this downregulation also results in decreased expression of CIDEB and APOB. This suggests a protective role for CIDEB against steatosis, rather than a mutation protecting from liver disease. Additionally, Cideb-deficient SLHs have reduced LD sizes and lower lipid exchange activities, indicating a loss of function is detrimental.

CDK9 is a kinase that regulates several physiological processes, including mRNA synthesis and processing by RNA polymerase II. It functions, along with T-type cyclins, as a component of P-TEFb to promote productive elongation during transcription and release paused RNA polymerase II. CDK9 activity is also involved in cell differentiation, and its deregulation has been linked to diseases like cancer and AIDS, as well as the replication of several viruses.

RBM24 regulates the cardiac QT interval through its control of CaMKIIδ splicing. Specifically, RBM24 ablation causes a shift towards the δ-C isoform of CaMKIIδ, which then activates the L-type Ca current. A short QT interval does not appear to increase the risk of mortality, and is more prevalent in males.

Yes, Rasch analysis supported the North Star Ambulatory Assessment as a reliable measure of ambulatory function in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, with a high Person Separation Index of 0.91. It also demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.935) and adequate reproducibility. Furthermore, it is recommended alongside other instruments for assessing activity and participation in muscular dystrophy.

Rilzabrutinib is an oral Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor. A study was conducted to define a clinically relevant supratherapeutic dose of rilzabrutinib and evaluate its potential effects on cardiac repolarization. The study involved administering rilzabrutinib alone and with ritonavir to healthy subjects to assess its impact on electrocardiogram parameters and pharmacokinetics.

Adult diffuse gliomas are genetically defined and include astrocytoma, IDH-mutant, oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q codeleted, glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, and, as of the 2021 World Health Organization classification, are further consolidated into astrocytoma, isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH)-mutant, oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant, and glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype.

Yes, several CAR T-cell therapies have been approved for treating B-cell malignancies. Tisagenlecleucel is approved for relapsed/refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, while axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel, and lisocabtagene maraleucel are approved or nearing approval for aggressive B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas. Additionally, Kymriah™ and Yescarta™ are approved CAR T cell therapies targeting CD19-positive cells in specific B-cell malignancies.

Yes, golodirsen is effective for approximately 8% of Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients amenable to exon 53 skipping, as it can restore the reading frame of the primary transcript and produce partially functional dystrophin, potentially slowing disease progression. Studies also demonstrate that golodirsen reduces pathological regeneration and increases dystrophin-associated proteins in treated patients.

Yes, baricitinib demonstrates efficacy in treating alopecia areata. Clinical trials show that, compared to placebo, oral baricitinib resulted in superior hair regrowth at 36 weeks in patients with severe alopecia areata, and a patient experienced striking improvement while on baricitinib treatment. Further investigation through clinical trials is warranted, but initial findings are encouraging.

No. Anemia is a major problem in patients with chronic kidney insufficiency and is often caused by the kidney’s inability to produce enough erythropoietin. Additionally, the cardio-renal anemia syndrome specifically links heart failure, kidney failure, and anemia, indicating a strong association.
The estimated reduction in time when using online genetic counseling is about 8%.

No. Evidence suggests patients receiving deflazacort experience similar or slower rates of functional decline compared with those receiving prednisone/prednisolone, indicating it is not *more* efficient, but comparable. One study showed statistically significant improvement with deflazacort in muscle strength measurements, but another states prednisone seems to have the potential for providing interim functional improvement while awaiting a cure.
Mosunetuzumab targets CD20 and CD3 to redirect T cells and eliminate malignant B cells.

Yes, anaphylaxis results from severe systemic mast cell activation and mast cells are considered primary cells responsible for driving anaphylaxis in humans. It can occur through IgE-mediated and other pathways, and even without a clear trigger, still involving mast cell activation.

Keytruda’s approval was based on a randomized, multicenter, open-label, dose-finding, and activity-estimating phase 1 trial enrolling 89 patients, and demonstration of objective tumor responses with prolonged response durations.

Yes, viltolarsen has been shown to increase endogenous dystrophin levels and has demonstrated long-term functional efficacy in patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy amenable to exon 53 skipping therapy, as evidenced by comparisons to a historical control group and continued evaluation of motor function. It is designed to skip exon 53 on the DMD primary transcript, which can treat 8-10% of DMD patients worldwide.

Wilkie's syndrome, also known as superior mesenteric artery syndrome, is a rare condition causing obstruction of the small intestine or upper gastrointestinal tract. This occurs due to compression of the third part of the duodenum between the superior mesenteric artery and aorta. A decrease in the aortomesenteric angle, often linked to a lack of subcutaneous tissue or recent weight loss, can precipitate this obstruction.

A Proteolysis-Targeting Chimera (PROTAC) is an innovative strategy that utilizes the cell’s own Ubiquitin-Proteasome System (UPS) to degrade target proteins. Each PROTAC molecule is composed of a ligand that recruits the protein of interest, a ligand specific for an E3 ubiquitin ligase enzyme, and a linker connecting these units. Upon binding, the PROTAC recruits the E3 ligase, inducing ubiquitylation and subsequent proteasome degradation of the target protein, and can be converted from existing inhibitors to enhance efficacy.

Yes, a “virtual digital twin” could guide nutrition in a personalized manner by combining genetic information with longitudinal metabolomic, immune, behavioral, and gut microbial parameters, and bioclinical variables. This model has the potential to revolutionize the management of obesity and support healthy aging, aligning with the core of preventative dietary interventions and the stratification of nutritional guidelines.

Teclistamab is a bispecific antibody that redirects T cells to target and kill myeloma cells. It achieves this by simultaneously binding to CD3 on T cells and B-cell maturation antigen on myeloma cells. This T-cell redirection results in T-cell-mediated cytotoxicity against the multiple myeloma cells, demonstrating promising efficacy in patients with relapsed or refractory disease.

The diagnosis of cholestasis is often suspected when elevations are seen in serum alkaline phosphatase activity, gamma-glutamyltransferase levels, and bilirubin concentrations, sometimes disproportionate to increases in serum aminotransferase levels.
Keytruda is produced by Merck, as the FDA previously approved it for treatment of several types of malignancies.

No, pioglitazone did not delay the onset of mild cognitive impairment. A study failed to meet the non-futility threshold and was terminated after finding no significant difference between those given pioglitazone and those given placebo.
Cereblon has been identified as the primary target of thalidomide and its analogs, such as lenalidomide and pomalidomide, which are immunomodulatory drugs.

No, silencing of SRRM4 suppresses microexon inclusion. It is demonstrated that SRRM4 is silenced in tumors, resulting in the suppression of normal microexon inclusion, and decreased SRRM4 expression is correlated with tumor growth.
AZD7442 is a combination of tixagevimab and cilgavimab, both long-acting monoclonal antibodies.
Nivolumab and pembrolizumab were the first two PD-1 inhibitors approved by the Food and Drug Administration in 2014.

Dermatillomania and skin picking disorder are considered to be the same condition, characterized by excessive and repeated skin picking that damages cutaneous tissue without an underlying dermatological disease and is classified under obsessive-compulsive and related disorders.
Japanese spotted fever is caused by a spotted fever group rickettsiosis and is a tick-borne zoonosis.

PD-1 is an immune checkpoint that inhibits the immune system when bound to its ligands, PD-L1 and PD-L2. PD-L1 and PD-L2 both bind to PD-1, but they differ in how they interact; PD-L1 exhibits a delayed interaction potentially due to a conformational transition, while PD-L2 does not. Both PD-L1 and PD-L2 can be blocked by an antagonist PD-1 antibody, enhancing T-cell proliferation.

A virtual digital twin could guide nutrition in a personalized manner by combining genetic information with various longitudinal health parameters. This model has the potential to revolutionize the management of obesity and related conditions, and support healthy aging. Preventative dietary interventions could exceed simple stratification and become highly individualized through the use of this technology. Ultimately, it could define a digital replica of oneself to personalize nutritional guidance.

No. Studies indicate levosimendan was not superior to placebo in maintaining respiratory function in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and sitting slow vital capacity was not significantly different between treatments. While some analyses showed a favorable effect on supine SVC, a larger study showed no significant difference between levosimendan and placebo regarding changes in supine SVC at 12 weeks or combined assessment of function at 48 weeks.

Clinical symptoms of ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency include vomiting, lethargy, hyperventilation, extreme irritability, ataxia, coma, delayed physical growth, developmental delay, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, encephalopathy, and protein avoidance, with symptoms potentially manifesting as early as the first week of life or as late as the sixth year, and sometimes presenting with an acrodermatitis enteropathica-like dermatosis.

SRSF11 is overexpressed in colorectal cancer and associated with a poor prognosis, playing a pro-metastatic role both in vitro and in vivo. It exerts these pro-metastatic effects by inhibiting the alternative splicing of HSPA12A pre-RNA. Specifically, SRSF11 regulates HSPA12A splicing by directly binding to a motif in exon 2, and phosphorylation by PAK5 protects it from degradation, further promoting metastasis.
Olokizumab is being tested for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis and shows promise in treating non-infectious uveitis.

Trophinin-associated protein (TROAP) functions as an adhesion molecule and regulates microtubules, initially identified in embryo transplantation and spindle formation. In hepatocellular carcinoma, TROAP suppresses cellular growth and migration, and its downregulation is associated with increased tumor cell growth. However, in glioma and lung adenocarcinoma, higher TROAP expression correlates with poor prognosis and reduced survival, indicating a complex role dependent on cancer type. Overall, TROAP expression appears to be linked to cancer progression and patient outcomes.

SRSF11 is overexpressed in colorectal cancer and contributes to a poor prognosis, playing a pro-metastatic role both in vitro and in vivo. It promotes metastasis by inhibiting the alternative splicing of HSPA12A pre-RNA, specifically by directly binding to a motif in exon 2. Furthermore, the oncogenic kinase PAK5 phosphorylates SRSF11, protecting it from degradation and enhancing its pro-metastatic effects.
Lenacapavir can be used for treating HIV-1, and is effective in both heavily treatment-experienced persons with multidrug-resistant HIV-1 and treatment-naive persons with HIV-1.

Yes, iron deficiency anemia is a common complication of chronic kidney disease. Several sources indicate it is frequent and clinically important, often resulting from decreased erythropoietin production and/or insufficient iron availability for red blood cell production. It is also associated with increased morbidity and mortality in these patients.

Yes, modulation of KCNQ1 splicing may help prevent arrhythmia, as it regulates IKs and action potentials with transmural differences and reduces arrhythmogenicity. Additionally, alterations in KCNQ1 are associated with long QT syndrome, which can lead to arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death.

Catamenial pneumothorax is a rare form of spontaneous pneumothorax in females and a frequent manifestation of thoracic endometriosis syndrome. It is characterized by a recurrent pneumothorax occurring within a day before or 72 hours after the start of menstruation. The condition is often unrecognized and underdiagnosed, and can sometimes be the first sign of endometriosis in the lungs and pelvis.

Haptenization is a process where liver proteins become modified, potentially leading to autoimmune hepatitis. This occurs when a combination of agents increases drug concentration, favoring the modification of proteins. It is hypothesized to happen in individuals with specific genetic variants affecting hepatic metabolism and susceptibility to oxidative stress. Ultimately, haptenization can trigger an abnormal immune response and the development of autoimmune hepatitis.

No, trichotillomania appears to be encountered with unequal frequency in males and females. A study of 38 individuals with trichotillomania included 35 women, suggesting a higher prevalence in females within the study population.
Eagle-Barrett Syndrome is characterized by a triad of deficiency or absence of abdominal wall muscles, malformations of the urinary tract, and bilateral cryptorchidism.

Neoplastic small lymphoid cells and small lymphocytes with slightly irregular nuclear contours proliferate in Mantle Cell Lymphoma, as well as mature, virgin B-cells characterized by a panB+, CD5+, CD23-, cyclin D1+ phenotype.
Telegenetics provides cost-effectiveness as a benefit, particularly for genetics services providing outreach clinics and patients who would otherwise travel long distances to access genetics.
BI 1015550 targets phosphodiesterase 4B, and it is a preferential inhibitor of this enzyme.

REMS and RMPs share similar objectives and toolkits for identifying, monitoring, and minimizing risk to patient safety. REMS requirements include patient medication guides, provider communication plans, and routine monitoring, which are not always included in RMPs. Conversely, RMPs may require specific adverse event reporting, prospective studies, additional trial data, and Summary of Product Characteristics contraindications that are not part of REMS. Both systems offer flexibility for product-specific actions based on potential adverse effects.

Yes, aberrations in microexon inclusion have been observed to lead to abnormal processes and dysregulation of the Rho GTPase pathway in central nervous system diseases. Microexons impact cellular physiology within the CNS, and mechanistic details illustrating their functional importance in these diseases is an area of ongoing research.
Beremagene geperpavec is tested for recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa.

Hairpatches is a single gene mutation associated with progressive renal disease and alopecia in mice, and may serve as a model for the rare human disorder glomerulonephritis with sparse hair and telangiectases.
Alternative microexon splicing by RBFOX2 and PTBP1 is associated with metastasis in colorectal cancer and dysregulation in the Rho GTPase pathway in central nervous system diseases.
Mutations in the gene encoding aspartoacylase (ASPA) result in Canavan disease.

No, prosopagnosia is a selective impairment of visual learning and recognition of faces, and is not described as a lack of auditory recognition. Some individuals with prosopagnosia may even demonstrate superior voice recognition abilities as a compensatory mechanism, and studies have shown that most subjects with developmental prosopagnosia are not impaired in short-term voice familiarity.

Telegenetics utilizes technology, typically video conferencing, to deliver genetic services remotely. It is particularly valuable for individuals facing limitations that prevent in-person clinic visits, such as those in rural areas. This approach offers benefits like cost-effectiveness and reduced travel time, while generally maintaining similar levels of knowledge and psychological outcomes compared to in-person services. Though generally well-received, some adaptations to counseling strategies may be needed to successfully implement telegenetics.

Mitapivat is an allosteric activator of the pyruvate kinase enzyme, and it is the first-in-class oral small molecule to function this way. It upregulates both wild-type and mutant forms of erythrocyte pyruvate kinase, which increases ATP production and reduces levels of 2,3-diphosphoglycerate. This activation leads to improvements in hemoglobin levels and a reduction in transfusion burden for individuals with pyruvate kinase deficiency and other hereditary hemolytic anemias.

Yes, necroptosis is characterized by plasma membrane permeabilization and disruption of cell membranes by MLKL after its activation, ultimately causing cell lysis. It is described as a form of regulated necrosis that involves plasma membrane disruption and is responsible for the inflammatory properties of the cell death process.

Dermatillomania is a pathologic grooming disorder involving repetitive, ritualistic, and impulsive skin picking. This occurs without an underlying dermatologic condition and can result in skin damage, distress, and impaired function. Treatment often involves addressing the underlying psychiatric illness, as well as treating any resulting pruritus and lesions.
Glofitamab can be used to treat relapsed or refractory B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma and diffuse large B-cell lymphomas (DLBCL), particularly after CAR T-cell therapy.

Concizumab is being developed for the treatment of hemophilia A and B, with and without inhibitors, and has been approved in Canada for adolescent and adult patients with hemophilia B who have FIX inhibitors and require routine prophylaxis to prevent or reduce bleeding episodes.
PMS2 germline mutations are associated with endometrial cancer and Turcot syndrome, in addition to colorectal cancers.

No. Obstructive sleep apnea is prevalent among stroke patients and is considered an independent risk factor for stroke, appearing in almost 50% of all stroke patients. It is also associated with poor functional outcome after stroke and a risk of recurrent stroke.

No, surgery timing can vary. While some babies require early closure surgery within the first 2-3 days after birth, prenatal repair is also an option, and can be performed at 26-28 weeks gestation, or even up to 30 weeks in certain situations. Additionally, advancements in treatment now include state-of-the-art intrauterine repair.

Oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy is a rare, progressive disease characterized by bilateral ptosis and dysphagia. It is a late-onset, autosomal dominant disorder that can lead to complications like weight loss, pulmonary infection, choking, and aspiration. The disease is caused by mutations in the gene encoding poly(A)-binding protein nuclear 1, specifically an expansion of a trinucleotide repeat, and results in muscle weakness beginning in adulthood.

Dersimelagon is an orally administered selective agonist for the melanocortin receptor 1. It increases levels of skin eumelanin and is being investigated for the treatment of erythropoietic protoporphyria, X-linked protoporphyria, and diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis. The drug demonstrates rapid absorption and a generally consistent pharmacokinetic profile across diverse subgroups.
The population attributable fraction of obesity in colorectal cancer development among women ranges from 0.8% to 12.3% for rectal cancer and 2.7% to 9.2% for colon cancer.

Yes, MDMA-assisted psychotherapy is effective in treating posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and is currently entering the final Phase 3 stage of drug development with a target for licensing by the FDA and EMA. Clinical studies investigating its use for PTSD have been deemed to have a favorable risk/benefit ratio by both the FDA and the Spanish Ministry of Health, and it has shown to enhance fear extinction learning, a key process in exposure-based therapies for PTSD.

A spillover event is the transmission of a pathogen from a vertebrate animal to a human. These events require ecological, epidemiological, and behavioral factors to align, as well as considering within-human susceptibility to infection. Spillover can be triggered by interactions between multiple species and often occurs at ecosystem boundaries where humans and wildlife have close contact. Identifying and averting spillover transmission is crucial for preventing outbreaks and mitigating infectious disease burdens.

Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy can be inherited in an X-linked or autosomal manner, and X-linked EDMD is caused by mutations in the EMD gene while autosomal EDMD results from mutations in the LMNA gene.

No. Valproate is a well-recognized teratogen and its use during pregnancy increases the risk of major congenital malformations, fetal valproate syndrome, and neurodevelopmental retardation. Adjusting or withdrawing valproate can also lead to deterioration of seizure control and status epilepticus, and higher doses are associated with a substantially higher rate of malformed offspring. Therefore, avoidance of valproic acid is strongly recommended during pregnancy.

A diet high in total lipid, saturated fatty acids, and monounsaturated fatty acids is associated with higher incidence of colorectal cancer, while dietary fiber, vitamin C, and traditional rice cake consumption are linked to lower incidence, and red and processed meat, bread, milk/dairy products, and beverages are positively associated with risk, alongside factors like haem and N-nitroso compounds found in red meat.

Electroporation is an effective method to enhance the immunogenicity of DNA vaccines, and optimizing its physical characteristics alongside construct design and delivery site is required to improve safety, tolerability, and the regulation of humoral and cellular responses.

Yes, measles vaccination programs have been among the greatest public health achievements, reducing measles incidence by 73% worldwide and decreasing measles-related deaths from over 4 million annually to an estimated 164,000 in 2008. Maintaining high levels of vaccine coverage is crucial, as outbreaks occur when population immunity is not sustained, and continued commitment to vaccination is supported by both direct and indirect benefits.

Golodirsen is a provisionally approved drug for approximately 8% of all Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients amenable to exon 53 skipping and can be used for DMD patients harboring out-of-frame deletion mutations in the DMD gene.
Trikafta contains elexacaftor, tezacaftor, and ivacaftor as active ingredients.
NTRK gene fusions are found at higher rates in colorectal microsatellite instability cancers, though rates in common cancers, including colorectal cancer, are consistently below 0.5%.

Tofacitinib, filgotinib, upadacitinib, GSK2586184, TD-1473, peficitinib, Pf-06651600/Pf-06700841, and deucravacitinib are Janus Kinase inhibitors being used or investigated for the treatment of Inflammatory Bowel Disease.
No, both children and adults are at risk of severe malaria. While severe pediatric cases have increased, severe cases are also increasing in adults, though fatalities are rare in both groups.

Corticosteroids are used to prolong ambulation in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, and analyses of patient practices regarding medication—including corticosteroids—are facilitated by patient registries like Duchenne Connect.

Cilta-cel was developed for the treatment of relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma in adult patients who have received at least four prior lines of therapy, including a proteasome inhibitor, an immunomodulatory agent, and an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody.

KRAS and TP53 mutations, as well as alterations in APC, are associated with in situ carcinoma evolution from colonic polyp adenomas, with KRAS mutations being more frequent in in situ carcinomas and villous adenomas compared to carcinomas, and TP53 mutation frequencies slightly lower in adenoma samples than in in situ carcinoma samples.

Prurigo nodularis is a chronic skin condition characterized by intensely itchy nodules, and it is often a frustrating disease to treat. It can present as disseminated nodules, often on all four limbs, associated with skin excoriations and intense pruritus. This condition may be autonomous, related to itch sensitization, and can significantly impact quality of life, sometimes even manifesting as a sign of underlying conditions like Hodgkin’s disease.
Human-related environmental disturbances are the driving forces behind the emergence of new human pathogens and increase the risk of spillover events.

Critical Care Pain Observation Tool and Behavioral Pain Scale are commonly used for pain evaluation in critically ill patients, while COMFORT and N-PASS scales appear effective for assessing pain and determining analgesic needs in mechanically ventilated neonates, and PIPP and CRIES scales are most often recommended for acute and postoperative pain.
The PEMMELA trial tested the combination of pembrolizumab, an anti-PD-1 antibody, and lenvatinib, an antiangiogenic multikinase inhibitor.

The microbiota can contribute to the development of colorectal cancer through colonic inflammation and dysbiosis, characterized by a decrease in beneficial bacteria and an increase in harmful ones. Alterations in the microbiota accelerate age-related DNA methylation drift in the colon, mirroring patterns observed in both aging and colorectal cancer. These changes affect gene expression and can lead to alterations comparable to those seen in colon cancer, potentially initiating or progressing tumor development.

Yes, carotenodermia can be caused by an excess of lycopene in the diet. One case presented involved a 21-year-old woman with orange palms and soles likely due to lycopenemia from a tomato-rich diet, and another study notes that, in contrast to other carotenoids, lycopene had no effect on liver preneoplastic foci.
The two main active ingredients of the standard PrEP are emtricitabine and tenofovir disoproxil fumarate.

Leigh syndrome is caused by DNA mutation, with approximately 75% of mutations occurring in nuclear DNA and 25% in mitochondrial DNA, and can also result from variants in genes like NARS2 or a mutation at nucleotide 8993 of mitochondrial DNA.

Primary diffuse meningeal melanomatosis is a rare tumor originating from melanocytes within the leptomeninges and represents a small percentage of central nervous system neoplasms. It can mimic other conditions and presents with a variety of neurological symptoms that develop over weeks or months, such as headaches, seizures, and cognitive impairment. Often, a definitive diagnosis is established postmortem despite comprehensive diagnostic procedures, and the average survival is approximately five months.

Yes, Black patients have worse survival in colorectal cancer. Multiple analyses demonstrate higher mortality rates and lower overall and cancer-specific survival for Black patients compared to White patients, even after accounting for various factors. Specifically, one study found a 15% higher chance of dying for Black patients with metastatic colorectal cancer, and another showed greater 5-year cancer-specific and overall mortality in Black patients with nonmetastatic colon cancer.

Yes, curcumin can kill tumor cells. It has anticancer effects and induces apoptosis in cancer cells via caspase activation and the production of reactive oxygen intermediates. Additionally, it can boost antioxidant defenses while not impairing tumor cell killing by radiation and may selectively kill bone cancer cells over healthy bone cells. It also increases the activity of natural killer cells, which directly kill cancer cells.

Yes, treatment should be individually tailored to the patient, focusing on preserving visual function and quality of life while minimizing side effects and socioeconomic burden. Lowering intraocular pressure is a key component of treatment, and early intervention, such as cataract surgery for specific conditions, may be preferable for glaucoma management. Additionally, combining pressure-lowering strategies with neuroprotective therapies may help preserve vision.

Pegozafermin’s mechanism of action involves activating the FGF21 signaling pathway through receptors found in tissues like the liver, adipose, skeletal muscles, and pancreas, which rely on beta klotho coreceptors. It promotes adiponectin levels, enhances insulin sensitivity, increases triglyceride uptake, and reduces de novo lipogenesis. This results in improvements in metabolic functions and reductions in liver fibrosis and inflammation.

Alirocumab is approved for the treatment of hypercholesterolemia in high-risk patients and is indicated for patients with heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia and those with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease who have substantially elevated LDL cholesterol despite maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe therapy.

No, despite over 30 years of research and extensive attempts to develop one, there is currently no approved vaccine against Helicobacter pylori. The information indicates that a vaccine would be beneficial due to increasing antibiotic resistance, but one is not yet licensed or available.

Tebentafusp is a treatment option for metastatic uveal melanoma, a highly fatal disease, and for previously untreated metastatic uveal melanoma in patients who are human leucocyte antigen-A*02:01 positive.
Havana Syndrome was first recognized in 2016 when diplomatic personnel in Havana, Cuba, began reporting neurological symptoms.

Yes, Marburg virus is a member of the same virus family as the Ebola virus, called Filoviridae. Several sources confirm this classification, noting both viruses belong to the Filoviridae family and cause severe hemorrhagic fevers.
The COSMIC-313 trial tested the triplet combination of cabozantinib, nivolumab, and ipilimumab against nivolumab and ipilimumab alone as a first-line treatment.

Domperidone functions as a dopamine D2-receptor antagonist and a peripheral dopamine receptor blocker, though it poorly crosses the blood-brain barrier. It increases circulating levels of luteinizing hormone in some women and exhibits gastrokinetic properties that contribute to anti-ulcer effects. Additionally, domperidone specifically binds to D2 dopamine receptors in the human caudate nucleus and can prevent the inhibitory action of apomorphine on copulatory behavior in rats.
The context does not identify any age group that should never be tested for HIV infection, but indicates that health-care providers should routinely screen all patients aged 13-64 years for HIV.

No, fingolimod is suspected to be harmful for fetal development and should be stopped at least two months before discontinuing contraception, and is not recommended during breastfeeding. It’s recommended to optimize MS treatment with nonteratogenic approaches when desiring pregnancy, and it has a known potential for teratogenicity.

Capnography measures carbon dioxide concentration or partial pressure of respiratory gas continuously and non-invasively, providing a measure of arterial partial pressure of carbon dioxide throughout the respiratory cycle.

Yes, men should be immunized against Human Papilloma Virus, particularly men who have sex with men, as they do not benefit from the herd protection gained through vaccinating girls. Vaccination of boys and girls is considered cost-effective for achieving herd immunity and reducing HPV-related malignancies in both sexes. Additionally, extending immunization offers to adolescent males and young men is an important opportunity for several countries, with a special focus on those at increased risk.

No. The provided information details associations between sectoral iris heterochromia and Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, Waardenburg's syndrome, 13q-syndrome, and Horner’s syndrome, as well as conditions like paravertebral neurilemmoma; Crohn’s disease is not mentioned in relation to this condition.

Lipectomy and liposuction are considered the most effective treatment methods for Madelung disease, though surgical intervention is the only effective therapy and the optimal approach is not yet fully determined.
